review.summary: Publishable with caveats. All mechanics, exact-artifact bindings, deterministic equality, role/dataset injection, and arithmetic pass. All four registered sources are accessible and support their core stated facts; S2-S4 only partially support the broader score bridges. The durable physical-service thesis remains useful, while adoption/capture scope and transaction-multiple proxies require visible qualification.

evidence.reviewer_note: Physical-trades anchor: do not convert licensed human field labor into AI substitution. All score-driving URLs above were reopened on the run date; source S4 is explicitly a public-company proxy, and the unsourced succession claim is treated as unconfirmed.

research_memo.executive_view: This is a durable-demand physical-services business, but not a large AI labor-arbitrage business. AI can take friction out of the office, selling and estimating stack; it cannot replace licensed technicians who diagnose, install and repair equipment at a building. The strongest buy-and-build case is recurring residential/light-commercial service, with caution on entry price and construction exposure. [S1][S2]
research_memo.how_ai_changes_work: The practical near-term tools are call handling, booking, dispatch, CRM updates, estimate follow-up, job summaries and marketing—not autonomous wrench work. ServiceTitan's contractor survey finds AI use concentrated in sales content, qualification and CRM work, while EPA certification remains required for refrigerant work. I therefore put high within-role exposure in clerical work and modest exposure in field roles. [S1][S2]
research_memo.value_capture: Flat-rate service menus and memberships can let a well-run local operator retain part of lower administrative cost, but customer acquisition, local competition and commercial bidding pass some savings through. The stated capture estimate deliberately excludes the technician/licensing floor: that floor protects demand, not the share of office savings retained.
research_memo.adoption_timing: AI is already visible in contractor software, but effective adoption means integrated workflows with clean price-book, CRM and dispatch data. The commercial contractor survey reports 29% use of AI agents, below the 50% boundary, so this packet uses a positive—not zero—time to 50% effective adoption. [S2]
research_memo.consolidation_economics: There are active strategic and sponsor-backed acquirers in construction services, but public-platform trading multiples are not small-shop transaction multiples. BizBuySell's sold HVAC businesses average 2.75x seller discretionary earnings; its universe is mainly local owner-operated businesses. A scaled, recurring-service exit can warrant more, but the exit estimate remains well below public construction-services trading marks. [S3][S4]
research_memo.terminal_demand: Terminal demand is DURABLE. A customer still needs physical diagnosis, installation and repair, and EPA Section 608 requires certification for covered refrigerant work. Smart equipment and remote diagnostics can reduce some visits or shift relationship ownership, but they do not turn a text model into a field technician. [S1]
research_memo.risks_and_uncertainty: The NAICS code mixes residential service, commercial mechanical contracting and new construction, while the supplied labor and target-count inputs are broad proxies. AI can also improve competitors' price books and lead conversion. The crucial uncertainty is how much 20–99 employee residential-service activity belongs in the EBITDA band, not whether AI replaces trade labor.

S1 and S6 support AI adoption and affected workflows but do not directly establish the AUM-fee prevalence or retention mechanism used in pi_dist and pricing_structure; those selections remain estimates.

S4's 11.0x median adjusted-EBITDA multiple comes from AGS's 37-transaction 2024 sample and is not a size- or quality-matched exit forecast; M is therefore highly sensitive to target normalization and transaction mix.
review.summary: Mechanics, exact identity/model routing, manifest digests, dataset injection, arithmetic, fresh finalization and fresh memo rendering all pass. All six registered sources are accessible. The direct adoption, adviser-population, deal-volume, buyer-count, assignment-consent and 11.0x adjusted-EBITDA claims are supported; remaining weaknesses are disclosed estimates or non-fatal source/scope limitations. No reversed factor semantics or capture/terminal double count is likely to change the thesis: client-consent risk is treated as a transferability constraint, and the PRESSURED terminal cap does not bind the current conditional score. The useful conclusion remains publishable only with all 18 authored caveats plus the two validator caveats, especially because M, n_band, owner age and capture can move the near-cut result.

occupations.title: Securities, Commodities, and Financial Services Sales Agents
Executive Secretaries and Executive Administrative Assistants

n_total.source: QCEW 2024 private annual avg establishments (SUSB out of scope for this code; establishments are a proxy for firms — overcounts multi-unit firms)

n_band.rationale: Start with 16,544 SEC advisers; 67.4% are below $1B AUM (11,151). Individual-client firms average $424M AUM; at a 0.8% realized advisory fee they generate about $3.4M revenue, and applying the supplied 11.65% margin yields about $0.39M EBITDA. The $1–10M EBITDA band therefore requires the upper part of the sub-$1B population and larger firms; 4,000 is a judgmental count allowing for higher AUM, fee and margin firms while excluding institutional managers and state-registered micro-firms.
caveats: Form ADV adviser census is not NAICS 523940 and excludes state-registered advisers.
The 0.8% fee and distribution within AUM bands are judgmental bridges; no published AUM-band table was reopened.
The supplied 11.65% margin is broad asset-management, likely below standardized wealth-RIA margins; n_band is intentionally low confidence.

caveats: Supplied role mix is 3-digit 523 and includes brokerage/asset-management contamination.

pi_dist.rationale: AUM-based fees decouple price from staffing cost, retaining some gains; fee compression and competition prevent full retention.
caveats: Fee schedules and client mix are target-specific.

pi_moat.rationale: Trust, planning complexity and contracts support retention, but AI-native advice and portable advisor relationships constrain it.
caveats: Client-consent rules create retention risk, not automatic moat.

t50_years.rationale: Use is widespread but most firms are experimenting; two years estimates the move to 50% effective, integrated workflow adoption.
caveats: Measured use is above 50%, but effective adoption is a stricter construct.

current_adoption_pct.rationale: 68% report some use and 63% of advisors report use, but both sources say use is early/experimental; 45% estimates effective task adoption.
caveats: Not a direct census measure; prevents incorrectly setting t50 to zero.

historical_analogs.value: Robo and digital-advice unbundling: routine allocation/reporting becomes cheaper while complex planning and trusted relationship service retain a paid segment.
historical_analogs.rationale: Sources show AI first affects administrative and communication work, consistent with unbundling rather than immediate replacement.
historical_analogs.plausible_range: qualitative
caveats: Not a causal estimate of future fee compression.

owners_60plus_pct.rationale: Small-adviser population creates succession opportunities, but IAA does not publish owner-age share in the reopened page.
caveats: No direct owner-age statistic; do not treat adviser demographics as owner demographics.

succession_shortage_documented.rationale: ECHELON reports a growing buyer universe and smaller firms moving toward partnerships, documenting succession/partnership demand rather than a numerical shortage.
caveats: This is market commentary, not a code-specific succession survey.

active_consolidators.rationale: Record deal volume, 17.6% more unique buyers and AGS's documented transaction-market competition support a high active-buyer count.
caveats: Count is an estimate of active platforms/strategics, not reported by either source.

buy_mult.rationale: Use an 8x adjusted-EBITDA entry estimate below AGS's 11.0x adjusted-EBITDA market median because the target is a founder-dependent $1–10M EBITDA business and requires owner-compensation and retention normalization.
caveats: AGS's transaction median is not a size-matched small-firm entry comp; quality small sellers may trade nearer the market median.
Owner compensation and contingent retention economics must be normalized before using this estimate in a live deal.

exit_mult.rationale: AGS's primary 2025 RIA Deal Room explicitly reports an 11.0x median adjusted EBITDA multiple for its 2024 RIA transaction data. Use that observed, denominator-matched market median as the exit benchmark, not a revenue, EBOC or undefined headline multiple.
caveats: The observed median spans RIA transactions and is not a size- or quality-matched forecast for a particular consolidated exit.
The report states larger and higher-quality firms receive higher valuations; a founder-dependent small target may require a discount after owner-compensation and client-retention normalization.

pricing_structure.value: Recurring AUM fees, often paired with planning/service, price advice as a percentage of managed assets rather than by labor hour; visible AI work increases fee-compression and unbundling risk.
pricing_structure.rationale: Schwab benchmarking covers products, pricing and technology, while AI evidence identifies the workflows clients may perceive as commoditized.
pricing_structure.plausible_range: qualitative
caveats: No exact fee-rate selection is used in scoring.

cross_checks.uplift_vs_A_consistent: High office/task exposure can be adopted faster than physical trades, but the positive two-year t50 recognizes regulated data, integration and trust constraints.

terminal_value.justification: Human advice retains trust and accountability value, but AI threatens the visible planning/communication layer and fee justification; contract assignment also makes revenue retention a real risk. [S5][S6]

cross_checks.no_proxy_confirmed: No: NAICS blends wealth advice and institutional asset management; role mix and labor share are broad proxies, and n_band is an explicit low-confidence fallback.
cross_checks.pricing_model_deflation: AUM pricing initially protects savings, but fee compression, AI-native substitutes and client portability can return savings to clients or acquisition competition.

top_uncertain_inputs.score_impact: Effective adoption versus experimentation changes the time penalty.
Fallback drives target-density/buyability and is not a NAICS census.
A move to the observed market median materially erodes multiple arbitrage.

evidence.reviewer_note: Attempt-2 remediation: replace the prior undefined 11x headline valuation with AGS's reopened primary report, which explicitly states an 11.0x median adjusted EBITDA multiple. The 8x entry estimate is now stated on the same adjusted-EBITDA basis and retains owner-compensation, retention and size-mismatch caveats. No EBOC or revenue multiple is treated as EBITDA. All listed score-driving URLs were reopened on the run date.

research_memo.executive_view: Independent wealth-advice RIAs offer meaningful AI productivity potential in planning preparation, client-service operations and investment workflow, but not a clean cost-free roll-up. Fees are tied to AUM rather than hours, helping retention of savings; at the same time buyers compete fiercely for transferable client books and client-consent rules make retention central. The code is heterogeneous, so this packet scores wealth-advice RIAs rather than institutional asset managers. [S1][S2][S5]
research_memo.how_ai_changes_work: AI can draft meeting notes, plans, emails, research summaries, service workflows and CRM updates. It is less able to replace relationship-based advice, behavioral coaching, fiduciary accountability and rainmaking. Schwab reports 68% of firms using AI in some way in its 2025 benchmarking study, while its later RIA-focused survey says use is concentrated in administrative tasks and client communication. [S1][S6]
research_memo.value_capture: AUM fees mean a reduced back-office cost does not automatically reprice the client contract, so a firm can initially retain part of savings. The counterweight is fee compression, robo/AI alternatives and portable founder relationships. pi_dist therefore reflects partial retention, and pi_moat reflects client trust and institutionalized service—not the regulatory human-accountability floor, which belongs to terminal demand.
research_memo.adoption_timing: The 68% broad AI-use result is above the mechanical 50% threshold, but it includes experimentation and does not establish 50% effective task adoption. The dedicated 2026 survey says most are still experimenting. I select 45% effective adoption and a positive two-year path to 50%, explicitly separating use from integrated savings. [S1][S6]
research_memo.consolidation_economics: Deal volume and the buyer universe are strong, which helps liquidity but weakens entry arbitrage. ECHELON reports record 2025 volume and more unique buyers. AGS's transaction report directly labels its 2024 11.0x market median as adjusted EBITDA, which makes it a denominator-consistent exit benchmark rather than a headline valuation statistic. It remains a broad transaction median, not a promise that a founder-dependent $1–10M-EBITDA purchase exits at that level; the 8x entry is a conservative estimate after matching owner compensation and retention economics. [S3][S4]
research_memo.terminal_demand: Terminal demand is PRESSURED, not melting. Human advisers retain value in complex planning, trust and accountability, and advisory contracts cannot generally be assigned without client consent. Yet AI makes a conversational substitute more credible and puts pressure on the visible, routine parts of a 1%-of-AUM proposition. [S5]
research_memo.risks_and_uncertainty: The dataset has three weak priors: ancestor-level labor share, 3-digit brokerage-contaminated role mix, and establishment count rather than firms. The required target-count fallback is a transparent estimate based on the Form ADV adviser census and small-firm distribution, not a NAICS census. M is fragile even after denominator repair: the observed 11.0x is an adjusted-EBITDA market median, while a small founder-dependent target may require a discount for owner replacement, client retention and cohort mix.

review.summary: Mechanics, identity, model routing, digest binding, deterministic dataset injection, fresh finalization, fresh memo rendering, and arithmetic all pass. All five registered score-driving sources are accessible. They support the useful direction of the workflow, licensing, consolidation, and valuation evidence, while the selected adoption, automation, capture, ownership, target-specific multiple, and future-exit judgments remain disclosed estimates or broad proxies. Factor semantics are not reversed, and no terminal/capture or ownership/consolidation reuse is likely to change the thesis: the no-re-rating 8.0x entry/exit case sets M to zero and independently preserves the kill verdict. The record is therefore publishable with its fifteen authored caveats.

evidence.reviewer_note: Attempt 2 remediates only retail-agency multiple support: two reopened Sica | Fletcher sources replace the non-comparable MGA benchmark, and the paired entry/exit estimate assumes no re-rating.

research_memo.executive_view: Retail independent P&C and benefits agencies have a real service-and-processing cost opportunity, but this is not a cheap roll-up. OPTIS counted 695 agency transactions in 2025 and leading buyers completed dozens each [S1]. I would buy only a commercial-heavy book with documented retention, carrier access and a disciplined price; AI is a margin enhancer, not a substitute for underwriting relationships.
research_memo.how_ai_changes_work: AI can reduce rekeying, certificate and endorsement handling, policy checking, renewal remarketing, carrier correspondence, reporting and first-pass quoting. Vertafore respondents expect the greatest help in data management, reporting, back-office efficiency and rating/quoting/underwriting [S2]. Producers, managers, adjusters and underwriters retain material judgment, negotiation and accountable client-facing work, so the replaceable share is deliberately partial rather than a headcount-elimination forecast.
research_memo.value_capture: Core agency revenue is commission-based, so lower service cost can remain with the agency more readily than in an hourly-services model; licensed producers are required to sell, solicit or negotiate insurance [S3]. Capture still leaks through carrier commission schedules, fee-based commercial accounts, producer portability and competitors using AI to lower service cost. Licensing is a modest operating moat, not a statutory protection of agency economics because carriers can distribute directly.
research_memo.adoption_timing: Effective adoption is below 50% today: Vertafore's 1,300-plus respondent survey says 23% expect AI to transform everyday work in 2026, 39% expect exploration, and 29% are waiting for proven results [S2]. Existing systems, carrier integrations, data quality and E&O controls slow end-to-end execution. A three-year path to 50% effective deployment is therefore more credible than treating stated optimism as current production adoption.
research_memo.consolidation_economics: Buyability exists in the supplied 3,236-firm band, but buyer crowding makes it scarce and expensive. OPTIS reports BroadStreet (69), Hub (49), Inszone (45), World (34), Keystone (32), ALKEME (27) and King Risk (22) among active 2025 buyers [S1]. Sica | Fletcher reports 11.8x average EBITDA for insurance-broker deals above $1M EBITDA through June 2025 [S4], while its agency-specific ballpark uses 8x and a 7-11x conservative range [S5]. I therefore use 8.0x at both entry and exit: it is a conservative retail-agency anchor, not an assumed multiple expansion; normalize contingent commissions and seller retention before any bid.
research_memo.terminal_demand: Terminal demand is DURABLE but pressured at the personal-lines and simple small-commercial edge. Complex commercial placement still needs carrier appetite navigation, renewal stewardship and a licensed intermediary, while simple transactions can increasingly migrate to direct or AI-native channels. The investment must underwrite the book mix instead of assuming commission renewals are permanently protected.
research_memo.risks_and_uncertainty: This NAICS code mixes retail agencies, benefits brokers, life shops, MGAs/MGUs and some captive activity; the analysis selects retail independent P&C/benefits agencies. The supplied wage mix is 5-digit while the dataset is 6-digit, the residual wage bucket is large, and Vertafore is a vendor survey rather than measured implementation. AI savings may require AMS/carrier integration spend and may be competed away before being retained.

review.summary: The exact attempt-2 packet, finalized run and memo pass schema, identity, routing, frozen-digest, dataset, deterministic-rerender and arithmetic checks. Both registered NAR sources are accessible and support the material reported adoption and consumer-agent-use facts. The 42% adoption input is a valid calculation from mutually exclusive reported frequency tiers, while its interpretation as effective firm adoption is candidly disclosed as a proxy. Several buyability, capture and multiple inputs remain low-confidence estimates rather than source-observed values, but their missing code-specific census, owner, transaction-comparable and pricing-series evidence is expressly caveated, ranges are provided, factor semantics remain correct, capture is separated from terminal demand, and the weak-acquisition conclusion remains useful.

evidence.reviewer_note: Attempt 2 remediates 2026-07-21_gpt-5.6-terra_v312p1_531210: the unsupported observed 41% claim is replaced by an explicit calculation from S1's daily and weekly use frequencies, with t50 reassessed against that effective-adoption proxy.

research_memo.executive_view: This is a weak AI-value acquisition despite meaningful workflow automation. The supplied wage base is small relative to gross commissions, while agent splits are contractual pass-throughs rather than controllable payroll. AI can make staff and agents more productive, but brokerages have fragile retention of both agents and leads, and post-settlement compensation remains in flux [S1].
research_memo.how_ai_changes_work: AI is already material in listing copy, marketing, research, follow-up and transaction administration: S1 reports 20% daily and 22% weekly AI use among surveyed REALTORS®, while 46% reported no noticeable business impact [S1]. We treat the 42% regular-use total, rather than all reported experimentation, as a proxy for effective operating adoption. AI can compress clerical and drafting work, not eliminate licensed negotiation, supervision, local judgment or trust in a high-stakes transaction. The supplied 3-digit role mix is contaminated by property-management and lessor jobs, so its physical-work shares are deliberately assigned low automation.
research_memo.value_capture: A brokerage retains only its company dollar after paying agent splits; productivity in an agent's personal workflow does not automatically enlarge that retained share. Technology can support pricing and service, but digital lead sources and MLS/portal channels limit office-level bargaining power. Consequently savings are likely shared with agents, spent on lead generation, or competed away; pi_dist and pi_moat are both deliberately low rather than treating commission pass-through as labor savings [S1].
research_memo.adoption_timing: S1 reports 20% daily and 22% weekly AI use; adding those source-stated frequencies yields 42% regular use [S1]. This is below the 50% effective-adoption boundary, while the additional 27% using AI a few times monthly and 46% reporting no noticeable business impact show that experimentation should not be equated with effective workflow change. A two-year estimate to 50% effective adoption is therefore a bounded judgment, not a source-reported forecast.
research_memo.consolidation_economics: The deterministic screen identifies only 259 firms in the indicated EBITDA band and warns that firm counts are dominated by very small shells. Acquisition economics are therefore thin and heterogeneous: agent books can move, owner compensation complicates EBITDA normalization, and retained-company-dollar economics cannot be compared with gross commission revenue. We estimate a handful of active strategic consolidators but do not use that fact as AI-adoption evidence.
research_memo.terminal_demand: Paid representation is pressured, not extinguished. NAR's 2025 buyer/seller survey reports 88% of buyers and 91% of sellers using an agent or broker, chiefly for negotiation, explanation, marketing and pricing [S2]. Those services survive AI, but AI reduces the information and paperwork component and makes a lower-fee licensed-execution model more plausible; this terminal-demand conclusion is separate from capture.
research_memo.risks_and_uncertainty: The key uncertainty is the mismatch between the 531 proxy occupation mix and a brokerage's actual payroll, compounded by rapidly changing compensation practices. NAR sources represent member respondents, not all brokerages, and regular survey use is only a proxy for effective operating adoption. The model also cannot infer a durable brokerage moat from consumer use of agents; portable agents, portals and franchise arrangements can absorb the value.

review.summary: All deterministic mechanics and frozen digests pass, and all six registered sources are live. The useful kill thesis is supported by observed legal GenAI adoption, documented workflow exposure, and binding ownership constraints. Publication requires the authored caveats because owner demographics, target-band transaction multiples, consolidator count, practice mix, and several economic selections rely on disclosed broad proxies or estimates; none is an inaccessible or contradicted score-driving claim that warrants rejection.

evidence.reviewer_note: Reopened sources establish adoption, workflow, ownership constraints and scope limits; target-band ownership and transaction multiples remain candid low-confidence estimates.

research_memo.executive_view: Law offices have substantial drafting, research, discovery and administrative task exposure, but this is a difficult conventional roll-up. The ABA Model Rule framework restricts nonlawyer ownership and fee sharing [S3], while client relationships are portable and owner compensation obscures EBITDA. I would view an acquisition as a lawyer-led succession transaction with carefully retained rainmakers, not a standard sponsor platform.
research_memo.how_ai_changes_work: GenAI accelerates legal research, writing, document review, matter management, pricing and billing workflows [S2]. Paralegal, secretarial, intake, records and first-draft tasks are most exposed. Lawyers retain court appearances, signing, advocacy, negotiations, client counseling and responsibility for outputs; their high wage share therefore cannot be treated as largely replaceable.
research_memo.value_capture: Hourly billing is the central leakage: faster production can mean fewer billed hours unless the firm holds price through value or fixed fees. Thomson Reuters reports law-firm GenAI use rose from 14% in 2024 to 26% in 2025 and 45% of respondents either use it or expect it central within a year [S1], making client expectations and competitive repricing material. Contingency practices retain a different, more cost-capture-friendly fee model.
research_memo.adoption_timing: Effective adoption remains below 50% despite rapid individual use: the 2025 Thomson Reuters study found 26% legal-space GenAI usage and 48% of law-firm professionals lacked formal policies [S1]. Governance, confidentiality, verification and practice-management integration slow reliable production use. I select a three-year t50 rather than calling general-tool usage a fully adopted workflow.
research_memo.consolidation_economics: The supplied target count overstates conventional buyability. Rule 5.4 bars nonlawyer partnership and nonlawyer ownership in the Model Rules [S3]; Arizona has an ABS regime that permits entities with nonlawyer economic interest or decision authority [S4], but that exception does not create a national roll-up path. The market data available for small firms is much smaller than the supplied $1-10M EBITDA band, so selected multiples are low-confidence and require transaction-specific owner-comp normalization.
research_memo.terminal_demand: Terminal demand is PRESSURED, not melting. Licensed human lawyers remain necessary for judgment, appearances and accountable representation, while simpler consumer documents and routine corporate work can be automated, self-served or moved in-house. AI is likely to compress billable hours before it eliminates the need for counsel, with outcome- and contingency-fee niches more resilient than commodity hourly work.
research_memo.risks_and_uncertainty: This code contains contingent plaintiff, insurance defense, corporate, consumer flat-fee and other practices with incompatible economics. The provided occupation mix is 4-digit 5411 and includes title-examiner contamination from sibling codes; it also misses partner draws. ABA population data covers lawyers broadly, not owners of target firms. The acquisition thesis is most exposed to rainmaker departure, ethics rules, UPL issues, fee-model deflation and unreliable AI outputs.

review.summary: Publishable with caveats. Exact identity, model routing, schema, dataset injection, byte-identical finalization and memo rendering, and arithmetic all pass. Census supports the corrected narrow 541120 boundary, and the registered sources support their expressly limited legal-authority, adoption-proxy and price-anchor facts. The zero-target conclusion remains useful as a low-confidence feasibility screen, not a measured market statistic: its office-count, revenue, margin, ownership and multiple inputs are openly judgmental, while the labor and adoption evidence are broad disclosed proxies. The state statutes also do not independently prove durable national paid demand, so the terminal label requires the additional validator caveat above. None of these limitations can reverse the kill thesis driven by zero B and M under the selected estimates.

n_band.rationale: Full feasibility chain, deliberately not a claimed census: estimate a generous 2,000 specialized 541120 offices nationally; use $0.25–1.0M revenue per office and a 20–30% EBITDA margin; modeled EBITDA is $50k–$300k per office, below $1M; therefore the selected estimated number of $1–10M EBITDA firms is 0. S1 confines the population to legal-document offices, while S2/S3 show the credentialed activity and S5 provides only an illustrative $105/hour document-preparation price. Lawyer offices, title/settlement operations and ordinary 541199 witnessing services are excluded rather than counted as 541120 scale.
caveats: No official national establishment, revenue, margin or transaction series was found for 541120; the 2,000-office population and revenue/margin assumptions are judgmental upper-bound inputs.
S5 is a single provider price list, not representative revenue, utilization or EBITDA evidence.

AI assists drafting and administration, while authorized approval, execution, archival and professional judgment remain human functions under the state models in S2 and S3.

pi_moat.rationale: The authority to draft, approve and execute documents, plus state-specific qualification and archival duties, is a real credential moat; limited geography, templates and substitute legal providers constrain it.

current_adoption_pct.rationale: Use the approximately 20% legal-specific AI implementation reported for firms with 50 or fewer lawyers as a conservative proxy, not as an observed 541120 penetration rate.

caveats: No historical code-specific analogue was found; this is directional only.

pricing_structure.rationale: The classification and state-law examples concern legal-document authority rather than a witnessing-only stamp; S5 illustrates time-based document-preparation pricing at one Louisiana provider.

cross_checks.uplift_vs_A_consistent: Task assistance is distinct from adoption: the legal-services proxy supports administrative and drafting automation, while the 20% current-adoption estimate and three-year t50 reflect constrained deployment in credential-sensitive work.

terminal_value.justification: The paid service remains durable while authorized humans are needed to draft, approve, execute and archive legal documents; Louisiana and Florida law assign those acts and qualifications to notaries [S2][S3]. AI pressures time and price, which is captured in C rather than counted as terminal disappearance.

cross_checks.no_proxy_confirmed: Ordinary notary-public witnessing, RON platforms, title/settlement offices and lawyer offices are excluded. Parent legal-services data are disclosed only as a labor/adoption proxy because the deterministic dataset provides no code-specific series.

top_uncertain_inputs.score_impact: A is 5.0 at three years, ranges from 5.0 at two years due to the cap to about 1.67 at six years.
B is zero at the selected value; any nonzero outlier count would raise buyability modestly but would not establish an investable market.
The 5411-parent mismatch makes V directional only and could move it materially.

evidence.reviewer_note: Attempt 2 remediates the prior material scope failure. Every score-driving source was reopened on 2026-07-21. The packet analyzes only 541120 legal-document offices and treats ordinary notary/RON evidence as out of scope.

research_memo.executive_view: 541120 is a narrow legal-document industry, not the ordinary mobile-notary or remote-online-notarization market. Census defines it as non-lawyer establishments empowered to draft, approve and execute legal documents, while assigning ordinary witnessing and signature certification to 541199 [S1]. Louisiana's civil-law notaries are the clearest domestic operating analogue: they may receive wills, conveyances and contracts, and their compliant acts are authentic acts [S2]. The corrected screen is still unattractive for a $1–10M EBITDA acquisition, but for a different reason: the addressable specialist-office population appears tiny and no code-specific evidence establishes a target-band company.
research_memo.how_ai_changes_work: AI can reduce document intake, first-draft, clause comparison, file organization, correspondence, scheduling and billing work. That maps to a meaningful share of the supplied 5411 legal-services proxy, especially paralegal and administrative roles, but it does not replace the state-authorized professional's approval, execution, recordkeeping or liability-bearing judgment. The ABA-hosted 2025 legal-industry survey reports uneven firm use of generative AI, including about 20% legal-specific AI implementation at firms with 50 or fewer lawyers, and use in drafting correspondence and operations [S4]. It is a legal-services proxy rather than a 541120 census.
research_memo.value_capture: The corrected product is a regulated professional service, so an authorized civil-law notary can retain some savings from faster intake and drafting rather than being limited to a generic per-signature fee. Louisiana law grants authority over contracts, conveyances and wills [S2], and Florida requires a civil-law notary to be a Florida Bar member with five years' practice before appointment [S3]. That credential and legal accountability create some moat, but a small office faces templates, legal-document software, lawyers and referrals as alternatives; savings will be shared through lower fixed fees and faster turnaround. No ordinary-notary fee cap or RON-platform take rate is used here.
research_memo.adoption_timing: The best available adoption evidence is a broad small-law-firm proxy, not a measure of 541120. The ABA-hosted survey found personal generative-AI use growing but only approximately 20% legal-specific AI implementation at firms with 50 or fewer lawyers, with ethics and workflow integration constraining deployment [S4]. I therefore estimate current effective adoption for the much smaller, credential-sensitive 541120 workflow below 50%, at 20%, and set a three-year path to 50%. This is deliberately slower than ordinary RON adoption because the relevant work includes legal-document approval and execution, not just identity verification.
research_memo.consolidation_economics: There is no usable code-level establishment, revenue or transaction series in the supplied dataset, and its n_total is zero. The fallback is therefore a feasibility estimate, not a market statistic: use a deliberately generous 2,000 specialized U.S. offices, $0.25–1.0M annual revenue per office, and 20–30% EBITDA margins, yielding roughly $50k–$300k EBITDA for the modeled typical office. The $105-per-hour document-preparation price shown by one Louisiana civil-law-notary provider [S5] is only an illustrative service-price anchor, not a revenue benchmark. On that chain, estimated $1M-EBITDA firms are zero, with a 0–3 outlier range for unusual multi-office practices; lawyer offices and title/settlement businesses are excluded by classification rather than used to manufacture scale.
research_memo.terminal_demand: Paid demand for authorized drafting, approval, execution and archival of legal documents is durable while state law reserves those acts to qualified people. Louisiana authorizes notaries to receive contracts, conveyances and wills [S2], and Florida ties civil-law-notary status to an experienced Bar member and grants authority to issue authentic acts [S3]. AI pressures labor time and routine-document pricing, but it does not itself hold the appointment, make an authentic act or bear the regulated professional role. The more credible terminal risk is margin compression and substitution toward lawyers, templates or software, not wholesale replacement by ordinary RON platforms.
research_memo.risks_and_uncertainty: This code is unusually difficult to size. The supplied labor share is 5411-parent data and the supplied role mix contains lawyers even though Census excludes offices of lawyers and attorneys from 541120 [S1]. Louisiana and Florida statutes demonstrate relevant authority but do not measure a national commercial-office population; the Louisiana price guide is one provider rather than a representative survey [S5]. Accordingly, every market-size, ownership, multiple and target-band estimate is low confidence. The primary diligence question is whether a specific candidate is truly a 541120 specialist office rather than a lawyer practice, title/settlement operation or ordinary 541199 notary service.

review.summary: All deterministic mechanics and exact-artifact checks pass. Every registered source is accessible; the central market, regulatory, attorney-opinion and valuation figures are present on the cited pages. The remaining weaknesses are disclosed AI/adoption and consolidation proxies, plus the thirteen authored caveats. The attorney-opinion mechanism is used for present order-flow ownership in C and long-run paid-demand pressure in the terminal classification, not as a demonstrated thesis-changing double count; in any event the independently supported no-expansion assumption sets M to zero and determines the kill verdict. The record is useful and publishable with caveats.

evidence.reviewer_note: Attempt 2 remediates the exact prior rejection: S5 was reopened and documents a title-and-escrow sample price and EBITDA basis. The 4.2x entry reference is directly stated; the same 4.2x exit is a transparent no-expansion estimate because no source supports a future spread.

research_memo.executive_view: Title and settlement agencies have material workflow automation potential in search, document production and processing, but they are mortgage-cycle sensitive and do not fully own their order flow. Regulated premium economics can retain some efficiency, while lender/realtor pressure, underwriter appointments and title-insurance alternatives constrain capture. [S1][S2][S3]
research_memo.how_ai_changes_work: Digitized-record search, data extraction, commitment drafting, order intake and post-closing are the AI target zone. Hard title defects, curative judgment, wire-fraud controls, state licensing and relationship-driven closing work remain human. The supplied 5411 occupational mix is law-office dominated, so it is an imperfect proxy for a title agency and is disclosed as such.
research_memo.value_capture: Texas shows the key split: title premium rates are promulgated, so cost reductions need not immediately change price, but the commissioner can reset rates; the regulator ordered a 6.2% basic-rate cut effective March 2026. Closing/escrow work is more exposed to lender and realtor fee pressure. [S2]
research_memo.adoption_timing: Title work has substantial digitization but effective AI adoption depends on county-record quality, underwriter integrations and fraud controls. I use 35% current effective adoption and a positive three-year time to 50%, not a claim that generic AI use equals completed workflow automation.
research_memo.consolidation_economics: Premium volume rebounded in 2025, and the market is governed by a concentrated underwriter ecosystem. A title-and-escrow transaction example reports a $2.6 million price on $620,000 EBITDA, or 4.2x; its broader guide gives a 3.0x–5.5x title-and-escrow range. The example is below the target EBITDA band, so it is a low-confidence anchor, but there is no matched evidence for a forward exit premium. The exit is therefore held at the same 4.2x reference and no multiple-expansion uplift is modeled. [S1][S4][S5]
research_memo.terminal_demand: Terminal demand is PRESSURED. Lenders may use qualifying attorney title opinion letters instead of a title policy, creating a real bypass channel, though it requires an attorney opinion and indemnity rather than eliminating title work entirely. [S3]
research_memo.risks_and_uncertainty: This NAICS code differs from title underwriters and attorney-closing states. The record's target count uses a broad margin and likely misses some 20–99 employee agencies. Mortgage volumes, county digitization, alternative title acceptance and upstream referral concentration are more decision-relevant than a generic legal-services wage mix.

evidence.reviewer_note: All score-driving sources were reopened. E-filing/e-service evidence is not mischaracterized as initial service of process; the U.S. Courts source expressly distinguishes that scope.

research_memo.executive_view: This catch-all is not one investable industry. Legal document preparation is highly AI-exposed and faces flat-fee platform competition; process service is physical but exposed to procedural e-service; USPTO practice has a registration gate but is a distinct specialty. I score the likely dominant revenue segment—document preparation and legal-logistics operations—with low confidence. [S1][S2][S3]
research_memo.how_ai_changes_work: AI can draft and assemble routine forms, classify files, route work, create status updates and support e-filing. LegalZoom's filing describes AI-enabled workflow automating complex legal/compliance processes at transparent flat fees. Physical service of process remains physical, while patent representation remains limited to registered practitioners. [S1][S4]
research_memo.value_capture: Per-document and per-serve flat fees have little structural price floor. A platform can retain scale economics, but fragmented operators face immediate customer comparison and search-platform pressure. Court integration and dispatch relationships may protect some legal-logistics revenue, but do not protect generic document preparation.
research_memo.adoption_timing: Current document automation is already mature; LegalZoom's platform combines AI workflow with human expertise. I set effective adoption at 50%, mechanically requiring t50 to be zero, while recognizing that local independent operators may lag the platform benchmark. [S1]
research_memo.consolidation_economics: Small-firm target supply is thin at the EBITDA band. ABC Legal actively offers to acquire process-service businesses and has national scale, but this is sparse evidence of a broad roll-up market and supplies no EBITDA multiple. The M selections are cautious estimates rather than platform headline values. [S2][S5]
research_memo.terminal_demand: Terminal demand is MELTING for generic document preparation, the scored dominant segment: AI platforms and guided e-filing reduce the need to buy form completion. Process service and patent practice retain demand for now, but their different protections do not rescue the catch-all's dominant low-moat segment. [S1][S3][S4]
research_memo.risks_and_uncertainty: The supplied 5411 role mix is law-office dominated and badly mismatched to this code. Revenue shares across document preparation, process service and patent-agent work are not directly sourced. E-filing is not the same as initial service of process, as the federal judiciary explicitly distinguishes that rule scope; this is why e-service risk is a terminal/regulatory caveat rather than an AI-adoption fact. [S3]

evidence.reviewer_note: All score-driving URLs reopened 2026-07-21; global adoption and 4-digit role scope are disclosed.

research_memo.executive_view: CPA offices offer meaningful AI-enabled labor productivity, but not an automatic windfall. Workflow automation is rapidly spreading [S1][S2], while attest sign-off, complex tax judgment and client relationships remain human. The best acquisitions will retain value through scarce talent and advisory positioning; commoditized compliance faces fee pressure.
research_memo.how_ai_changes_work: AI can accelerate audit preparation, tie-outs, research, tax drafts, client accounting and administrative throughput. CPA.com emphasizes workflow automation with human-in-the-loop verification [S2], which supports partial—not full—replacement of professionals. The supplied 5412 mix includes tax preparation, payroll and bookkeeping siblings, so it is a disclosed proxy rather than a CPA-only wage map.
research_memo.value_capture: Savings may be retained where firms sell advice and complex outcomes, but hourly or hours-anchored work invites clients to demand lower bills as preparation time falls. AICPA reports technology/AI change as the leading expected issue across firm sizes, and experienced-staff hiring remains a major issue for 11–30 professional firms [S3], supporting some retention but not a monopoly.
research_memo.adoption_timing: Wolters Kluwer reports 41% AI adoption globally among tax and accounting firms in 2025, 35% daily use and 77% planning more investment [S1]. Because this is global and tool use is not effective deployment, a positive three-year estimate to 50% effective adoption is more candid than treating current experimentation as complete.
research_memo.consolidation_economics: The deterministic data identifies 2,286 firms in the target EBITDA band, a real but competitive acquisition universe. CPA ownership, independence and partner-compensation normalization complicate capital structures. Active consolidators and higher-quality platforms exist, but deal activity is used only for buyability and multiples, not as evidence that AI tasks are replaceable.
research_memo.terminal_demand: Terminal demand is durable for attest and complex tax/advisory, where professional accountability and client judgment remain valuable; routine bookkeeping and simple compliance are pressured. AI thus changes the staffing model and mix of paid work more than it eliminates a well-positioned CPA firm. This demand conclusion is distinct from whether the operator captures efficiency.
research_memo.risks_and_uncertainty: Adoption evidence is global, while the deterministic labor share is direct but 2022-vintage and the occupational mix is 4-digit. Partner draws sit outside payroll and broker-style revenue multiples can mix owner compensation with EBITDA. Fee realization and practice-sale statistics were not reopened, so capture and multiple estimates are deliberately low-confidence ranges.

review.summary: All deterministic mechanics and frozen digests pass, and all three registered sources are live. IRS Free File and H&R Block's filed channel, AI-assistance, franchise, and refund-product evidence support the useful conclusion that simple consumer preparation is highly automatable but offers weak capture and consolidation economics; GAO confirms Direct File is suspended. Publication requires the authored caveats because adoption above 50%, role weights, independent-firm economics, owner demographics, consolidator count, and multiples are disclosed estimates or broad proxies rather than code-level observations. No inaccessible, contradicted, or materially misrepresented score-driving evidence warrants rejection.

evidence.reviewer_note: All score-driving URLs reopened 2026-07-21; current Direct File status is documented as suspended, not assumed expanded.

research_memo.executive_view: Consumer tax preparation is a poor AI migration target despite high automatable labor. Simple returns face free guided software and AI-enabled DIY options, while only 40 deterministic firms reach the $1–10M EBITDA band. Storefront value rests more on trust, refund-related products and complex exceptions than form completion [S1][S2].
research_memo.how_ai_changes_work: Interview, intake, document extraction, return preparation, status calls and seasonal scheduling are highly automatable. H&R Block nevertheless sells assisted preparation with guarantees and additional refund products [S2], showing that a human-service layer remains for some customers. The supplied 5412 role mix is CPA-dominated and does not represent seasonal preparers, so the apparent precision of V is explicitly limited.
research_memo.value_capture: Flat per-return prices compete with free alternatives. IRS Free File has eight private-sector partners for 2026 and offers eligibility up to $89,000 AGI [S1], while Direct File itself is suspended for the 2026 season [S3]. Labor savings are therefore likely competed away in simple returns; refund transfers, advances and related services can retain value but are product/regulatory dependent [S2].
research_memo.adoption_timing: The core workflow is already software-mediated and H&R Block markets AI assistance, so effective automation for simple-return production is estimated above 50%, making t50 exactly zero. This does not mean every franchise or independent has fully automated intake or that all customers will self-serve.
research_memo.consolidation_economics: The deterministic band count is only 40 and comes from 100–499 employee firms, not typical single storefronts. H&R Block franchisees pay approximately 30% of gross tax-preparation and related-service revenue as royalty [S2], illustrating why franchise resale economics cannot be treated as independent-practice EBITDA. A local acquisition may not own the brand, client relationship or product economics.
research_memo.terminal_demand: Terminal demand is melting for simple paid 1040 preparation: the government form, free filing options and increasingly capable software remove much of the reason to pay. Demand can remain for complex, immigrant/ITIN, dispute-resolution and refund-product niches, but these are narrower and not a license-protected broad service. This is separate from AI's labor-saving effect.
research_memo.risks_and_uncertainty: Direct File was suspended rather than expanded, which weakens one disruption channel but does not remove Free File or commercial DIY pressure [S1][S3]. The code's seasonal workforce, owner SDE and franchise royalty loads make annual margin comparisons fragile. No reopened independent fee study or age census supports precision in pricing, ownership or succession inputs.

research_memo.executive_view: Payroll processing has abundant AI/RPA task exposure, but local bureaus face scale platforms and embedded fintech. Regulatory liability and money movement preserve an intermediary, though often the platform rather than the acquirable bureau [S1].
research_memo.how_ai_changes_work: Payroll platforms are investing in generative AI and automate routine compliance, calculation and client workflows [S1]. Human exception handling, tax notices and client relationships remain; the supplied CPA-heavy occupation mix omits payroll clerks and is a material proxy caveat.
research_memo.value_capture: PEPM recurring pricing can retain automation gains, but ADP’s recurring-revenue platform, long retention and client-funds capabilities show the scale advantage local bureaus face [S1]. Competitive platforms can pass savings through, so capture is moderate rather than assumed.
research_memo.adoption_timing: Platform payroll is already digital and ADP reports generative-AI investment [S1]. Effective automation is estimated above 50%, so t50 is zero, although independent bureaus may lag the platforms.
research_memo.consolidation_economics: The deterministic screen has 297 target-band firms, but small bureaus face fixed compliance costs and channel dependence. Active platform consolidation is not used as AI-adoption evidence and multiples remain low-confidence.
research_memo.terminal_demand: Paid payroll intermediation is durable because multi-state remittance, custody, reporting-agent responsibility and exception handling remain costly. The local bureau is pressured by platform oligopoly, which is distinct from the survival of payroll processing.
research_memo.risks_and_uncertainty: Labor share is unusually high and compares different vintages; revenue can net client funds. The supplied 5412 mix is not payroll-specific. No reopened independent-bureau price or transaction census supports precise capture or multiples.

ai_replaceable_share.rationale: Bookkeeping/write-up and billing workflows are task-automatable, while review, cleanup, exception handling, and relationship work remain. The frozen 5412 role mix is a CPA-heavy proxy, so the residual judgment reflects the prompt's bookkeeping/billing-dominant hypothesis rather than a claimed measured wage mix.
caveats: No 541219-specific occupational wage census was reopened.
The supplied role mix is 4-digit 5412 and misstates the bookkeeper/biller concentration of the selected subsegment.

breakdown_by_role.rationale: Non-CPA accounting production can be assisted, but review and client judgment remain.
Categorization, transaction entry, reconciliation, and close support are core automation targets [S2].
Tax preparation is adjacent rather than dominant and retains client-specific interpretation.
Reporting and analysis are assisted, while control and advisory accountability remain.

breakdown_by_role.role: Secretaries and Administrative Assistants, Except Legal, Medical, and Executive
breakdown_by_role.rationale: Scheduling, document chasing, and routine communications automate readily.
Owner management and client retention are only partly automatable.
General office data handling and follow-up are workflow-automation targets.
AI can prepare analysis, but operating recommendations require judgment.
Billing, statements, and invoice workflows are highly structured [S3].

breakdown_by_role.rationale: Supervision remains necessary for exceptions and quality control.
Residual is treated as bookkeeper/billing-heavy under the disclosed code-specific hypothesis, tempered for cleanup and review work.

pi_dist.rationale: Fixed monthly fees can retain part of hours saved, but transparent native-platform pricing and widespread automation limit durable pass-through.
caveats: QuickBooks Live is a national platform benchmark, not an independent 541219 price census.

pi_moat.rationale: Incumbent trust and messy-books knowledge help, but the dominant core has no CPA licensure moat and the platform owns the ledger and managed alternative.
caveats: Some non-CPA accounting and medical-billing subsegments have different moats.

t50_years.rationale: Rapid workflow adoption supports a near-term path, but daily AI use and process automation are not the rubric's 50% effective task-replacement metric for 541219; three years is a judgmental bridge from the disclosed current 30% estimate.
caveats: No 541219 census measures effective AI replacement.
This is intentionally positive because current effective adoption is below 50%.

current_adoption_pct.rationale: Broader surveys show heavy tool use, but their definitions do not establish that half of the selected industry's production work is effectively automated; 30% is a conservative effective-adoption estimate, not a restatement of survey usage.
caveats: S3 covers accounting professionals, not code-matched non-CPA bookkeeping firms.
S4 covers SMB clients across sectors, not providers.

historical_analogs.value: Embedded ledger workflow automation is spreading from assistance and review toward recurring transaction-processing tasks, while paid commitment remains narrower than regular AI use.
historical_analogs.rationale: The sources document current process/tool adoption and the gap between regular use and paid commitment; they do not provide a dated 541219 adoption curve.
historical_analogs.plausible_range: Adoption may accelerate or stall with trust, accuracy, and liability concerns.
caveats: Not a longitudinal 541219 analog.

owners_60plus_pct.rationale: No reopened owner-level age evidence matches non-CPA bookkeeping/write-up firms, so this is a neutral low-confidence planning assumption and receives no succession bonus.
caveats: Not evidence of an owner-age census.

succession_shortage_documented.rationale: No code-matched, reopened documentation establishes a succession shortage.
caveats: False means unproven, not disproven.

active_consolidators.rationale: No reopened platform-by-platform evidence establishes active 541219 bookkeeping/write-up add-on consolidators within the required window; assign no buyability credit rather than recycle payroll, CPA, or VC evidence.
caveats: Does not assert that no acquisitions occurred; it records no qualifying documented evidence in this packet.

buy_mult.rationale: A conservative normalized EBITDA planning value is used only to avoid false precision: no reopened, accessible 541219 $1-10M EBITDA transaction set supplies a defensible SDE-to-EBITDA normalization.
caveats: Not an observed transaction multiple.
Small practice listings often quote revenue or SDE with owner compensation inside.

exit_mult.rationale: A modest scale premium is a low-confidence scenario assumption, not a claimed $10M-plus EBITDA 541219 comparable; platform price pressure and weak consolidation evidence constrain it.
caveats: No reopened exit comp matches the rubric's $10M-plus EBITDA and selected-service scope.

pricing_structure.value: Recurring monthly fixed fees, frequently tiered by bookkeeping volume or expenses, with cleanup/onboarding work separately priced.
pricing_structure.rationale: Intuit's Live Bookkeeping publishes a cleanup/onboarding first-month fee and ongoing monthly expense-tier pricing.
pricing_structure.plausible_range: Independent firms may also use hourly, monthly, or hybrid billing.
caveats: Observed pricing is a platform benchmark, not a census of independent firms.

cross_checks.uplift_vs_A_consistent: Task automation is high, but current effective adoption is deliberately below 50% and t50 is positive; adoption speed is supported by workflow evidence rather than ownership or consolidation.

terminal_value.justification: Cleanup, review, and advisory remain paid work, but the ledger platform automates and sells the recurring bookkeeping core [S2].

cross_checks.no_proxy_confirmed: Payroll-only, CPA-only, tax-only, and generic SMB evidence is not used to claim 541219 ownership, consolidation, or transaction facts; broad accounting/SMB sources are labeled as adoption/task proxies.
cross_checks.pricing_model_deflation: The $200/$400/$600 QuickBooks Live tiers are modeled in capture via pi_dist and pi_moat, not as terminal-demand preservation or adoption evidence.

top_uncertain_inputs.score_impact: A ranges from 10.0 at one year to 1.67 at six years; it is 3.33 at the selected three-year midpoint.
V remains capped at 10 across this range because the frozen labor-share input is 0.7934.
M is highly scenario-dependent; selected values produce a modest 0.67 score, not a sourced market claim.

evidence.reviewer_note: Remediation attempt 2 corrects the material scope failure: 541219 is researched as other accounting/bookkeeping/write-up/billing, not payroll-only 541214. Every score-driving cited page was reopened on 2026-07-21; ownership, consolidator, and multiple evidence remains intentionally uncredited where code-matched support was not available.

research_memo.executive_view: 541219 is not a payroll-bureau code: Census excludes businesses providing payroll services only and instead includes non-CPA accountant, bookkeeper, and billing offices [S1]. I score bookkeeping/write-up and monthly-close work as the dominant operating thesis. AI can remove substantial production labor, but native ledger automation and Intuit's competing managed-bookkeeping offer make the likely outcome a pressured, not a durable, roll-up proposition.
research_memo.how_ai_changes_work: The core service is transaction entry, categorization, reconciliation, accounts payable/receivable processing, and management reporting—not payroll remittance. Intuit's Live service itself includes bank connections, categorization, and reconciliation [S2]. In the related accounting-professional survey, data entry/processing was the leading AI client-service use case (50%) and invoicing/payments the leading internal use case (44%) [S3]. Human review, cleanup of messy source data, client explanations, and controller-level judgment remain meaningful.
research_memo.value_capture: Monthly fixed-fee bookkeeping can initially retain hours saved, but its price umbrella is unusually exposed: Intuit sells ongoing Live Bookkeeping at $200, $400, or $600 per month after a $500 cleanup/onboarding month, based on client expense volume [S2]. That is a visible national benchmark for the same recurring close/reconciliation work. I therefore assign modest distribution retention and a weak moat: switching friction and trust help an incumbent, while a zero-licensure core and platform-owned ledger constrain price and client ownership.
research_memo.adoption_timing: The accounting-professional survey reports 46% daily AI use and 95% process automation, but it covers a broader, tech-forward accounting population rather than 541219 firms and measures tool/process usage rather than 50% effective replacement of the target's work [S3]. I do not convert those figures into a claim that this code has already crossed the rubric's effective-adoption threshold. A 30% current-effective-adoption estimate and three-year t50 are a cautious bridge from rapid embedded workflow rollout, with the timing score explicitly low confidence.
research_memo.consolidation_economics: The frozen screen's 531 $1-10M EBITDA candidates are a mechanical size proxy, not a validated local-bookkeeping deal universe; it is concentrated in 100+ employee classes while most code establishments are much smaller. I found no reopened, code-matched evidence of active add-on consolidators, owner-age share, or a succession shortage, so the buyability score intentionally receives no consolidator or succession credit. The book can be transferable when staff and client records survive the seller, but this packet does not turn that judgment into a scored ownership fact.
research_memo.terminal_demand: Paid demand should survive for cleanup, controls, exception resolution, client communication, and higher-value outsourced-controller support. However, the most repeatable portion of the dominant service—monthly categorization, reconciliation, and reporting—is both automated inside the ledger and offered as managed service by the ledger owner [S2]. That creates a different channel from labor savings: the platform can eliminate or reprice the independent firm's product. I classify terminal demand as PRESSURED rather than MELTING because the residual work is real but narrower and less standardized.
research_memo.risks_and_uncertainty: This catch-all code also contains billing offices, medical billing/payment services, non-CPA accounting, and some bundled tax/payroll work; their credentials, client stickiness, and AI exposure differ materially [S1]. The frozen 4-digit 5412 wage mix is CPA-heavy and understates bookkeeper/biller concentration, while the labor and target-count datasets are fixed inputs rather than re-estimated here. There is no accessible, size-matched 541219 transaction set or owner-demographic census in the reopened sources, so multiples, owner age, and consolidation are deliberately low-confidence estimates rather than false precision.

review.summary: Publishable with caveats. Exact schema, identity, model route, frozen digests, dataset injection, fresh finalization, memo rendering, and arithmetic all pass. All ten registered sources reopened and support the directional task, adoption, licensure, relationship, transaction, and valuation evidence. The kill thesis remains useful and no factor semantics or material terminal/capture double count is reversed; publication must preserve the authored proxy limitations plus the two non-fatal provenance and ROG-statistic corrections.

labor_share.method: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 541320 ($2,653,798,071) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 541320 ($5,788,521,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

ai_replaceable_share.rationale: Observed use is concentrated in early design, reports and RFPs, so production/documentation roles receive partial rather than majority automation fractions; accountable field and management work remains low. The 4-digit occupation mix is a disclosed proxy.
caveats: The supplied 5413 role mix is engineering-dominated rather than 541320-specific.
Survey use is task- and respondent-based, not a measured labor-hour replacement study.

breakdown_by_role.rationale: Reports, concepts and documentation are partly AI-assisted; licensed engineering judgment, approvals and site responsibility remain human. [S1][S2]
Conceptual and report work is AI-assisted, but accountable design and client judgment are not shown as automated. [S1][S2]
RFP, report and coordination documentation can be assisted; client and agency coordination persists. [S1][S2]
Production and visualization have more exposure, but the cited surveys show early design and concepts rather than autonomous construction-document output. [S1][S2]
Administrative drafting is assisted, while operating decisions and client relationships remain human. [S1]
Documentation assistance is plausible, but technical accountability and coordination remain human. [S1][S2]
AI can assist reports and proposals, not managerial responsibility or professional accountability. [S1]
Physical inspection and site observation have little direct support for replacement in the cited task evidence. [S1][S2]
Some documentation and analytical assistance is plausible; licensed technical work remains human. [S1][S2]
Tooling can accelerate coding tasks, but the source does not support full replacement. [S1]
Unlisted wage share likely contains mixed design support and administration; selected below a majority because observed use remains early-stage. [S1][S2]

pi_dist.rationale: Direct-client and consultant contracting routes coexist; I select 40% retained savings because direct relationships can retain some productivity while consultant work and competitive proposals reprice it. No cited source quantifies the mix.
caveats: ASLA contract forms demonstrate relationship types, not fee method, price realization or revenue shares.

pi_moat.rationale: Practice acts can protect licensed performance, but title acts and overlapping/incidental practice limit the breadth of the moat. The selected value does not assume a state count or a measured regulated-fee share.
caveats: No accessible source in this packet quantifies 541320 revenue inside the regulated practice perimeter.

t50_years.rationale: At the selected 25% effective baseline, the survey's 77% trial-and-error learning and 15% billable-experimentation permission indicate that routine, value-realizing firm deployment is still immature; four years to 50% is a bounded forward estimate.
caveats: No landscape-specific longitudinal effective-adoption series was located; this is not derived from a CAD/BIM timeline.

current_adoption_pct.rationale: Effective adoption is mapped to routine applied use with a reported workflow benefit: 27% reported time saved, versus 55% reporting any use across practice, teaching or research. I select 25% after discounting the mixed respondent pool and experimental use.
caveats: The ASLA survey is not a census of US 541320 firms and its time-saved measure is respondent-reported.

historical_analogs.value: No landscape-architecture-specific CAD/BIM adoption series was validated. The adoption estimate instead uses the current survey distinction between broad AI use and reported time saving; no historical technology cadence is imported.
historical_analogs.rationale: The current task and workflow observations are relevant to adoption maturity; they do not substantiate a BIM/CAD historical analog.
historical_analogs.plausible_range: Not a numeric analog
caveats: No dated landscape-specific CAD/BIM adoption observations were reopened.

owners_60plus_pct.rationale: No owner-level age evidence was reopened, so this remediation assigns no owner-age bonus rather than carrying forward an unsupported estimate.
caveats: Zero is a scoring-conservative placeholder, not a claim that no owners are over 60.

succession_shortage_documented.rationale: No accessible landscape-architecture succession survey was reopened; no succession-shortage bonus is applied.
caveats: Absence of evidence in this packet is not evidence of no succession issue.

active_consolidators.rationale: Three distinct buyers announced acquisitions of explicitly landscape-architecture targets within the 24 months preceding 2026-07-21: Greenprint/Omni, ISG/Lift and Populous/OJB.
active_consolidators.plausible_range: 3 observed named buyer/target transactions; not a census of consolidators
caveats: The announcements do not disclose target EBITDA, consideration, recurring-platform strategy or PE backing for every buyer; only Populous' separate PE minority investment is verified in S8.

buy_mult.rationale: No consideration or target EBITDA is disclosed for the three landscape transactions. I use 3.5x as a conservative screening entry estimate near the 3.35x lower quartile of ROG's mixed all-firm A/E sample, not as an observed landscape transaction multiple.
caveats: ROG covers mixed architecture, engineering and environmental transactions, including internal minority interests; it is not a 541320 target-comps series.

exit_mult.rationale: Three current strategic landscape deals and the Populous/OJB buyer-capital evidence support buyer interest, but not a price. I select 4.5x, below ROG's 5.30x all-firms median and within its 3.35x–5.38x all-firms range, as a bounded exit screening estimate.
caveats: No cited landscape-architecture transaction reports a paid multiple or establishes an eligible $10M-plus EBITDA platform exit.

pricing_structure.value: The accessible evidence confirms both direct landscape architect-client and landscape architect-consultant contractual routes; their fee mix is unmeasured in this packet.
pricing_structure.rationale: Use the verified contract categories to describe route-to-market heterogeneity and avoid claiming an unsupported lump-sum, hourly or sub-consultant share.
pricing_structure.plausible_range: Relationship types observed; revenue mix unknown
caveats: No accessible LA-specific billing or prime/sub-consultant revenue-share survey was reopened.

cross_checks.uplift_vs_A_consistent: A 25% effective-adoption baseline and four-year t50 are consistent with task use concentrated in early workflow stages and only 27% reporting time saved; the labor uplift is theoretical rather than a current realized saving. [S1][S2]

terminal_value.justification: Accessible ASLA and CLARB evidence supports licensed practice protection and overlapping/incidental adjacent practice, respectively. This preserves accountable work but does not establish a protected revenue share; discretionary work is therefore pressured. [S3][S4]

cross_checks.no_proxy_confirmed: No: the supplied 4-digit 5413 occupation mix is engineering-contaminated and does not represent a code-specific landscape-architecture wage mix.
cross_checks.pricing_model_deflation: Relationship types are verified, but their revenue mix is not. pi_dist is a conservative estimate that allows direct-client retention and consultant/competitive pass-through without claiming a sourced billing split. [S5]

top_uncertain_inputs.score_impact: A faster conversion of early-stage AI use into routine time-saving work raises A and accelerates realized V.
Undisclosed landscape-deal pricing makes M a bounded screening estimate, not a transaction-comps conclusion.

evidence.reviewer_note: Attempt 2 remediates the prior fleet findings: the inaccessible exemptions PDF is replaced by reopened ASLA and CLARB pages; effective adoption is explicitly mapped from broad use to reported workflow benefit; three named landscape-architecture deals and one verified PE-backed buyer are supplied; unsupported CAD/BIM, owner-age, succession, billing-mix and deal-price claims are removed or bounded as estimates.

research_memo.executive_view: Landscape Architectural Services has real AI leverage in research, reports, concepts and RFP work, but the sector is still experimenting rather than operating AI as a mature production system. Current acquisition evidence shows strategic buyer interest, not a liquid small-firm platform market. The investable core is licensed, accountable site work; discretionary design and principal-held relationships make the rest pressured. [S1][S2][S9]
research_memo.how_ai_changes_work: The best directly observed landscape-architecture evidence is task-specific: ASLA reports AI use in writing reports (45%), conceptual design (41%) and RFP responses (29%), while IFLA reports research/writing/predesign as the leading uses. That supports partial automation of documentation and visualization, not autonomous stamping, agency coordination, site observation or design accountability. The supplied 5413 occupation mix remains an engineering-heavy proxy. [S1][S2]
research_memo.value_capture: ASLA's standard forms explicitly contemplate both a landscape architect-client agreement and a landscape architect-consultant agreement. That establishes two economically different routes to market but does not quantify their revenue mix. I therefore treat retention as a bounded judgment: direct-client firms can retain some early productivity, whereas consultant work and competitive proposals can reprice it. Licensure protects who may perform regulated work in practice-act jurisdictions, not the price of every design output. [S3][S4][S5]
research_memo.adoption_timing: I define effective adoption as routine applied use that has produced a reported workflow benefit, rather than any use in teaching, research or experimentation. In ASLA's more-than-300-response 2024 survey, 55% reported some AI use but only 27% reported time saved; the respondent pool was mixed, with 51% at landscape-architecture firms, and only 15% of firms allowed billable experimentation. I select 25% effective adoption after excluding the broad-use and experimental component, and estimate four years to 50%; this is a transparent mapping, not an observed forecast. [S1]
research_memo.consolidation_economics: There are three directly documented U.S. buyer/target transactions inside the 24 months before this run: Greenprint acquired Omni Workshop (January 2025), ISG acquired Lift Environmental Design (July 2025), and Populous acquired OJB Landscape Architecture (May 2026). Populous had disclosed a Providence Equity Partners minority investment before the OJB deal; I do not infer PE backing for the other buyers. Consideration and target EBITDA are undisclosed, so the entry and exit multiples are deliberately bounded screening estimates anchored only loosely to ROG's mixed A/E transaction distribution. [S6][S7][S8][S9][S10]
research_memo.terminal_demand: Terminal demand is PRESSURED, not melting. ASLA states that title acts restrict the title while practice acts restrict unlicensed performance of the work, and CLARB notes overlapping or incidental practice with architecture, engineering, interior design and surveying. This supplies an accessible, authoritative explanation for why accountable regulated work can persist while discretionary or adjacent-scope work remains contestable. No source located here quantifies the fee pool inside that protected perimeter, so this is not treated as a measured moat. [S3][S4]
research_memo.risks_and_uncertainty: The core uncertainties are the contaminated 5413 role mix, the absence of a landscape-specific effective-adoption time series, unknown direct-client versus consultant revenue mix, undisclosed consideration in the three landscape deals, and no reopened owner-age/succession dataset. The score therefore gives no owner-age or succession bonus and uses low-confidence, explicitly bounded valuation estimates rather than importing unsupported A/E precision. [S1][S6][S7][S9][S10]

review.summary: All deterministic mechanics, exact-byte digests, identities, route, dataset injection, fresh finalization, memo rendering, and arithmetic pass. Reopened sources support the useful direction of AI task compression, partial savings pass-through, limited workflow/data capture, adoption timing, active A/E acquisition, valuation ranges, and durable accountable demand. The record separates licensure and responsible review as terminal-demand mechanisms from pi_moat, so no score-changing terminal/capture double count remains. Publication nevertheless requires the authored caveats because several score inputs rely on disclosed broader-AEC proxies, unquantified contract mix and capture, an unobserved owner-age/shortage estimate, and adviser or paywalled valuation evidence.
