ai_replaceable_share.rationale: S1 and S2 establish an output that mixes design production with contract administration, procurement, and installation support. S3 directly shows current AI use in routine design-firm operations. Fractions are task judgments for the locked supplied roles: visual/document production and administrative work are more replaceable than client interpretation, site work, contractor coordination, and physical logistics.
caveats: The supplied mix is four-digit 5414 and carries graphic-design/art-direction contamination from sibling code 541430.
The 37.39% residual is a bounded judgment rather than a reweighted six-digit occupational structure.
S3 measures business-tool use among Houzz design respondents, not observed task-level automation of 541410 wage roles.

breakdown_by_role.rationale: Plans, visualizations, schedules, and specifications are AI-assisted, while client discovery, code-aware judgment, sourcing, contractor coordination, and site oversight remain human work.
Graphic production, presentation, mood-board, and marketing asset tasks are highly exposed to generative tools, although final brand and client review remain human.
Operations reporting, internal planning, and routine communications are automatable; ownership, staffing, client escalation, and commercial accountability remain.

breakdown_by_role.role: Sales Representatives of Services, Except Advertising, Insurance, Financial Services, and Travel
breakdown_by_role.rationale: Lead research, outreach drafts, and proposal materials automate, while relationship selling and trust-based conversion do not fully.
Schedules, follow-ups, budget reporting, and routine project records automate; contractor coordination, substitutions, and site exceptions persist.
Document formatting, calendars, billing support, and routine file work are prime current AI applications, while office presence and exception handling remain.

breakdown_by_role.role: Laborers and Freight, Stock, and Material Movers, Hand
breakdown_by_role.rationale: Physical receiving, moving, inspection, and handling of furnishings are minimally automated; only notices and paperwork are exposed.

breakdown_by_role.role: Market Research Analysts and Marketing Specialists
breakdown_by_role.rationale: Market research summaries, content generation, campaign drafts, and analysis are highly AI-assisted, with positioning and channel judgment retained.

breakdown_by_role.role: Secretaries and Administrative Assistants, Except Legal, Medical, and Executive
breakdown_by_role.rationale: Routine correspondence, meeting summaries, document handling, and administrative tracking are AI-exposed; executive support and sensitive coordination remain.
Concept exploration and presentation production automate materially, but creative direction, client taste translation, and approval retain human responsibility.

The unlisted remainder likely combines junior design/CAD, purchasing, bookkeeping, expediting, and installation-support roles; it is modeled as a balanced mix of automatable information work and physical coordination.

pi_dist.rationale: Mixed billing creates mixed retention. Flat-fee scope can retain faster production, hourly fees pass the time saving to the client through fewer billable hours, and procurement markup is less tied to design hours. I select a 40% retained-savings share before moat.
caveats: S4 provides practitioner examples rather than a representative target-size revenue split.
This models pass-through only; it does not claim that product procurement or human coordination is an AI labor saving.

pi_moat.rationale: Full-service work includes construction administration, specifications, product ordering, installation oversight, contractor coordination, and client satisfaction. These create a meaningful relationship and execution moat, but it is weakened when the principal’s personal taste and network originate the revenue.
caveats: Sources establish the service bundle, not a measured firm-level retention or pricing-moat rate.
Licensing varies by state; this estimate does not use regulation as a blanket moat.
Principal portability is a separate deal-level risk that may lower the value for residential boutiques.

t50_years.rationale: Nearly one-third of design firms already use AI and 50% of firms with 10+ employees report use. Because the relevant $1–10M EBITDA targets are larger than the median respondent but complex client tasks lag, I estimate 2.5 years to 50% effective workflow adoption.
caveats: S3 measures any AI tool use among Houzz design-firm respondents, not a 541410 target-band measure of effective task adoption.
This timing does not use succession, market size, transaction activity, or the expected terminal-demand class.

current_adoption_pct.rationale: Houzz reports nearly one-third of design businesses use AI. I map that directional result to 32% current effective use, below the 50% boundary because the report says the sector is early-stage and use is concentrated in routine tasks.
caveats: The numeric mapping is a judgment from a qualitative ‘nearly one-third’ statement and the survey is not restricted to NAICS 541410.
The target pool may have higher basic tool use, but that does not necessarily imply higher effective use in client-facing design work.

historical_analogs.value: The current analog is progressive workflow digitization rather than a completed displacement event: BLS records CAD/BIM as standard design production, while Houzz shows AI first entering low-risk administration, scheduling, document formatting, and content generation before complex client management.
historical_analogs.rationale: The sources support a phased workflow pattern: digitized drawing and visualization plus early AI in routine work. The inference that AI moves from routine work toward integrated production is stated as an analogy, not observed causal history.
historical_analogs.plausible_range: A gradual augmentation path is better supported than sudden end-to-end replacement.
caveats: Neither source is a longitudinal 541410 productivity study or a direct estimate of future task displacement.

owners_60plus_pct.rationale: ASID’s succession research explicitly addresses interior-design firm owners nearing retirement but supplies no owner-age distribution. I use 30% as a cautious proxy for the share over 60 rather than inventing a code-specific age statistic.
caveats: S6 studies succession planning in 63 architecture and interior firms under 40 employees, not owner ages, NAICS-only firms, or the $1–10M EBITDA band.
This demographic proxy is used only for buyability, not adoption or capture.

succession_shortage_documented.rationale: ASID/Greenway documents low formal succession-plan rates and a leadership gap among the sampled architecture and interior design firms, which is direct documentation of a succession-planning shortage in the relevant professional-services context.
succession_shortage_documented.plausible_range: true/false; true for documented planning and leadership gaps, unmeasured for all 541410 firms
caveats: The study is limited to 63 firms under 40 employees and combines architecture with interior design; it does not measure seller willingness or target-quality supply.

active_consolidators.rationale: Chambers is one verified strategic acquirer of an interior-design studio. I count one verified active strategic buyer rather than asserting a broad roll-up wave or a complete market census.
active_consolidators.plausible_range: 0–3 verified strategic acquirers in the visible public evidence
caveats: S7 proves one acquisition in private-club planning/interiors; it does not establish a PE platform, a national consolidator count, or buyer interest in ordinary residential studios.

buy_mult.rationale: A 2025 interior design-and-build transaction was appraised at 3.2x trailing EBITDA [S8]. I use 3.2x as an estimated entry anchor, below the broker-published 4x–6x directional range [S9], because typical targets may have founder dependence and less favorable repeat revenue than the disclosed hybrid commercial example.
caveats: S8 is one NYC-metro commercial design-and-build deal with repeat revenue, approved-vendor status, and remodeling exposure; it is not a standalone 541410 comp set.
S9 is broker marketing guidance, not a disclosed transaction database.
The frozen dataset’s receipt-based margin estimate includes product pass-through and should not substitute for target-level normalized EBITDA.

exit_mult.rationale: I estimate a slightly lower exit multiple than entry because a future buyer will underwrite more visible AI pressure on design fees and the same founder/client portability risk. The observed 3.2x hybrid transaction and 4x–6x broker range are context, not evidence of this future exit.
caveats: This is a forward underwriting estimate, not an observed forecast or a claimed multiple-expansion thesis.
Service mix, client concentration, procurement margins, and retention of creative principals can move the actual exit materially.

pricing_structure.value: Mixed flat design fees, hourly billing, cost-plus/procurement management fees, and product markups; full-service firms may also bill for contract administration and furnishings resale.
pricing_structure.rationale: S1 formally distinguishes full-service, design-only, consulting, and resale products. S4 supplies current practitioner examples of flat fees and fee-plus-markup structures. Together they support a mixed, non-standard pricing description.
pricing_structure.plausible_range: Revenue mix varies materially by residential versus commercial work and by whether furnishings flow through the firm.
caveats: S4 is not a representative target-band revenue survey, and S1 is a product taxonomy rather than a financial dataset.

cross_checks.uplift_vs_A_consistent: Yes. Task replaceability is based on design-production and administrative work. The 2.5-year adoption estimate is supported separately by current design-firm AI use and the fact that complex client-management use lags routine adoption [S3]; it does not borrow from owner succession or M&A evidence.

terminal_value.justification: The code includes full-service administration, procurement, installation-related coordination, and furnishings resale [S1], while BLS describes real product, contractor, code, and site work [S2]. Those channels preserve paid demand, but AI can commoditize design-production artifacts and pressure design fees and product transparency.

cross_checks.no_proxy_confirmed: No. The role mix is broad 5414, Houzz is a platform sample of design-only firms, ASID’s succession study combines architecture and interiors, pricing evidence is practitioner-level, and the EBITDA comp is a single design-and-build transaction. Each proxy is identified in the affected caveats.
cross_checks.pricing_model_deflation: AI’s effect on flat, hourly, and procurement-markup models is assigned to pi_dist. The task score does not assume pricing deflation, and the human physical-work floor is discussed only in terminal demand rather than treated as capture.

top_uncertain_inputs.score_impact: A ranges from 10.00 at one year to 2.50 at four years; the source records tool adoption, not full fee-earning workflow replacement.
C changes materially with the target’s mix of hourly fees, flat fees, and procurement/product economics.
C depends on whether the relationship, trade accounts, systems, and senior team belong to the firm rather than the departing principal.
M is sensitive to normalization of product pass-through, target mix, and the lack of a broad standalone-541410 transaction set.

evidence.reviewer_note: All score-driving evidence was live-reopened. The packet does not claim a code-specific AI adoption rate, owner-age census, target revenue mix, consolidator census, or transaction database where none was verified. Estimates are deliberately bounded and caveated; actual acquisition underwriting requires target-level service/product gross margin, client-transferability, principal-retention, and project-pipeline evidence.

research_memo.executive_view: Interior Design Services is a selective, pressured AI opportunity rather than a clean automation roll-up. The code includes planning/design as well as construction-contract administration, specifications, and furnishings-related resale [S1]. BLS likewise describes a workflow spanning client discovery, layouts, material specification, ordering, installation oversight, contractor coordination, and site visits [S2]. AI can reduce substantial visualization, documentation, proposal, marketing, and administration effort, but the supplied low labor share (0.2359) means that labor savings apply to only part of receipts: physical goods and procurement flow through the business. The practical investment question is whether a target has a transferable commercial client base, procurement system, and senior team, rather than a founder’s residential taste and contacts. I assess reasonable but not exceptional value creation, moderate retention, an already-active adoption curve, and PRESSURED—not melting—terminal demand.
research_memo.how_ai_changes_work: AI exposure is concentrated in creating and revising plans, mood boards, renderings, presentations, marketing, schedules, basic specifications, and office workflows. BLS confirms that designers use CAD for most drawings and BIM for three-dimensional visualization [S2]. Houzz’s 2025 survey of US design-only firms reports that AI is used primarily for document formatting, scheduling, and content generation, while complex client management is less adopted [S3]. That maps to partial, rather than complete, replacement of interior designers: a designer still translates a client brief, checks codes and dimensions, specifies real products, resolves substitutions, coordinates contractors, and visits the site. Graphic-design and administrative roles carry higher task fractions; physical movers and receiving remain low. The four-digit 5414 mix is contaminated by graphic-design activity, so the higher fraction assigned to those supplied roles is a task judgment, not a claim that graphics is proportionally central to a pure interior-design firm.
research_memo.value_capture: Whether the firm keeps AI savings depends on its revenue model. Trade reporting describes firms using flat fees, hourly work, cost-plus procurement, and product markups, with no standard fee structure [S4]. For a well-scoped flat-fee design phase, faster production can be retained. For hourly work, fewer hours mechanically reduce revenue; for product-markup and procurement fees, AI drawing speed has limited direct effect while direct-to-consumer shopping can weaken the furniture-margin base. BLS’s task list shows enduring relationship, procurement, and coordination work [S2], which supports a real service moat, but not necessarily a firm-level moat when the principal owns the client relationship. I therefore give a midrange distribution share and moat: the score does not treat the physical work floor as captured value, and it does not use adoption timing to inflate capture.
research_memo.adoption_timing: I estimate 32% current effective AI adoption and 2.5 years to 50% effective adoption in the relevant target population. Houzz found nearly one-third of design businesses already using AI, rising to 50% among firms with at least 10 employees [S3]. The frozen target pool begins at the 20–99 employee class, so its firms are plausibly ahead of the all-design average, but the survey measures any everyday business use, not automation of fee-earning design work. Houzz also reports that designers use AI first in routine administration and content, with complex client management lagging [S3]. The selected timing therefore recognizes meaningful current use without equating a chatbot or document tool with full design-process adoption. It is independent of owner age, target count, or M&A evidence.
research_memo.consolidation_economics: This code is fragmented, but the frozen dataset identifies 313 firms in the target EBITDA band, a much more usable starting pool than the many sole practitioners below it. ASID’s 2025 succession research covered 63 architecture/interiors firms under 40 employees and found formal succession plans at only 30% of 25–40 employee firms, 12% of 2–10 employee firms, and under 5% of sole practitioners [S6]. That documents a real transition problem, though it does not give an owner-age rate or a buyer-ready target count. There is at least strategic acquisition activity: Chambers acquired C2 Limited, an interiors/planning firm, while retaining its brand and leadership [S7]. This is not evidence of a broad PE roll-up. A 2025 design-and-build transaction was appraised at 3.2x trailing EBITDA [S8], but it had commercial repeat revenue, approved-vendor status, and remodeling exposure, so it is an optimistic hybrid comp. I use that as a restrained purchase anchor and underwrite a modestly lower exit multiple because acquisition success depends on retaining creative leadership and client relationships.
research_memo.terminal_demand: Terminal demand is PRESSURED, not MELTING. The formal product taxonomy distinguishes full-service design, which includes construction administration and construction documents, from design-only and consulting work [S1]. It also identifies furnishings resale margins as a related product [S1], explaining why labor cost is a low share of receipts and why AI-generated images alone do not erase the business. BLS’s workflow includes product orders, installation oversight, contractor coordination, code awareness, and site follow-up [S2]; those are paid physical-world and accountability functions. Yet client-accessible visual ideation, online product discovery, and low-friction AI content can commoditize the early design phase and erode product-markup transparency. Demand should survive for well-positioned full-service commercial, hospitality, healthcare, and high-touch residential firms, but their mix is unknown and design-fee-only firms face more disintermediation.
research_memo.risks_and_uncertainty: Evidence is useful but mismatched to the exact acquisition target. Houzz’s sample is US design-only professionals registered on its platform and includes residential and commercial design; it is not a census of 541410 firms or a measure of task-level replacement [S3]. The supplied role mix is the broader 5414 group, so graphic-design wages likely overstate their share in the code. Business of Home articles illustrate practitioner pricing but are not a representative revenue-mix survey [S4]. The specific 3.2x EBITDA transaction is one design-and-build business with favorable commercial characteristics [S8], whereas a broker’s 4–6x general range is marketing guidance rather than disclosed comparables [S9]. Finally, 541410 bundles residential, commercial, contract-administration, decorating, procurement, and resale activity; value capture and terminal demand differ sharply across those lines. Low confidence is therefore intentional, and a real diligence case should reconstruct service fees, product margins, founder-originated revenue, client concentration, trade-account economics, and employee retention before assigning a multiple.

records.title: Industrial Design Services

publication_caveats: inputs.ai_replaceable_share: The supplied 4-digit 5414 role mix is contaminated by sibling-code interior and graphic design and omits Commercial and Industrial Designers.
inputs.ai_replaceable_share: The 0.3739 residual wage share carries the core product-design, engineering, prototype, and other unlisted work; its fraction is a broad hypothesis.
inputs.ai_replaceable_share: S1 is a qualitative 20-person interview study and S2 is broad design research, neither a representative 541420 task-time survey.
inputs.ai_replaceable_share: High task exposure does not assume a studio can retain the savings; pass-through is scored only in pi_dist and pi_moat.
inputs.pi_dist: S3 is one Canadian consultancy's commercial explanation, not a US industry pricing survey.
inputs.pi_dist: This selection addresses savings pass-through only; it does not assume that external demand survives AI.
inputs.pi_moat: S4 describes a larger, broad-service example and should not be generalized to all 541420 firms.
inputs.pi_moat: The same founder dependence also depresses exit multiples, but the distinct channel here is current competitive retention of operating savings.
inputs.t50_years: S1 is qualitative and S2 is broad design research with a startup-heavy adoption finding, not a representative 541420 adoption curve.
inputs.t50_years: IP policy, client confidentiality, CAD interoperability, and training may make smaller studios slower or faster than the selected midpoint.
inputs.current_adoption_pct: Neither source measures US lower-middle-market industrial-design task displacement.
inputs.current_adoption_pct: Individual designer use should not be equated with a firm realizing headcount or contractor-cost reductions.
inputs.historical_analogs: This is a qualitative analogy, not a measured history of CAD, rendering, or industrial-design employment.
inputs.historical_analogs: Tool quality may advance faster than client approval, organizational policy, or product-liability acceptance.
inputs.owners_60plus_pct: Founder age and ownership are not inferable from professional-designer demographics or general small-business surveys.
inputs.owners_60plus_pct: This input needs a code- and size-specific owner survey before it can support acquisition sourcing claims.

review.summary: Mechanics, exact identity, frozen digests, deterministic dataset injection, fresh finalization, memo rendering, and arithmetic all pass. All seven registered sources reopened and support the limited claims made. The thesis remains useful, but the extensive authored caveats correctly identify broad or foreign proxies, a contaminated occupation mix, an anomalous labor-share construction, and weak code-specific ownership and valuation evidence.

ai_replaceable_share.rationale: Fractions credit AI for visual ideation, rendering, research synthesis, routine documentation, proposals, scheduling, and administrative production, while withholding credit for embodied prototypes, user research, client leadership, and design-for-manufacture judgment. The core occupation is absent from the supplied 4-digit mix, so the residual uses a cautious blended judgment rather than treating sibling-code graphics roles as a direct industrial-design measurement.
caveats: The supplied 4-digit 5414 role mix is contaminated by sibling-code interior and graphic design and omits Commercial and Industrial Designers.
The 0.3739 residual wage share carries the core product-design, engineering, prototype, and other unlisted work; its fraction is a broad hypothesis.
S1 is a qualitative 20-person interview study and S2 is broad design research, neither a representative 541420 task-time survey.
High task exposure does not assume a studio can retain the savings; pass-through is scored only in pi_dist and pi_moat.

breakdown_by_role.rationale: Interior Designers are a sibling-code contamination but have exposed visualization, layout, variants, and documentation work; site context, client choice, and implementation coordination remain human.
Graphic Designers have highly exposed production graphics, image variants, pitch assets, and routine content work, while direction, brand judgment, and client review remain.
Operations managers retain client leadership, hiring, creative accountability, and commercial judgment; reporting, planning, and internal coordination are addressable.

breakdown_by_role.role: Sales Representatives of Services, Except Advertising, Insurance, Financial Services, and Travel
breakdown_by_role.rationale: Proposal drafts, account research, and follow-up can be automated, but relationship selling and discovery of an ambiguous product problem remain human.
Schedules, risk logs, status reporting, and budget tracking are addressable; trade-off arbitration across client, engineering, and creative work remains.
General office records, invoice support, routine data movement, and correspondence are highly addressable, subject to controlled client records and review.

breakdown_by_role.role: Laborers and Freight, Stock, and Material Movers, Hand
breakdown_by_role.rationale: Material movement and hands-on support are physical tasks; only simple labels, routing, and associated documentation are addressable.

breakdown_by_role.role: Market Research Analysts and Marketing Specialists
breakdown_by_role.rationale: Market-research synthesis, case-study drafting, campaign variants, and routine analysis are exposed, while market judgment and client positioning remain.

breakdown_by_role.role: Secretaries and Administrative Assistants, Except Legal, Medical, and Executive
breakdown_by_role.rationale: Administrative coordination, contract paperwork preparation, calendaring, and invoicing support are highly addressable, with supervisory and exception work retained.
Art Directors can automate visual exploration and production variants, but retain aesthetic strategy, critique, client facilitation, and final direction.

The residual is expected to include core industrial/product designers, engineering, model-making, research, and production-support roles. It receives credit for CAD, rendering, specifications, and analysis support but not for prototyping, field research, manufacturability, or final product judgment.

pi_dist.rationale: A studio can retain some savings within an already signed fixed-price or staged milestone, but exploratory work is commonly fee-for-service and later milestones, renewal, scope definition, and procurement expose savings to re-pricing.
caveats: S3 is one Canadian consultancy's commercial explanation, not a US industry pricing survey.
This selection addresses savings pass-through only; it does not assume that external demand survives AI.

pi_moat.rationale: A broad integrated studio can retain some advantage through research, engineering, prototypes, and client knowledge, but a typical small studio has no reviewed licensing gate and faces client insourcing, founder dependence, and comparable AI tools.
caveats: S4 describes a larger, broad-service example and should not be generalized to all 541420 firms.
The same founder dependence also depresses exit multiples, but the distinct channel here is current competitive retention of operating savings.

t50_years.rationale: The midpoint reflects fast individual tool adoption in design but slower conversion into controlled, client-approved, CAD-connected, and repeatedly labor-saving studio workflows. It is not a forecast of full autonomous product development.
caveats: S1 is qualitative and S2 is broad design research with a startup-heavy adoption finding, not a representative 541420 adoption curve.
IP policy, client confidentiality, CAD interoperability, and training may make smaller studios slower or faster than the selected midpoint.

current_adoption_pct.rationale: Twenty percent represents effective adoption against the selected replaceable task share, not survey respondents who have tried an AI tool. The evidence supports rapid grassroots use and uneven organizational integration, so the estimate remains well below 50%.
caveats: Neither source measures US lower-middle-market industrial-design task displacement.
Individual designer use should not be equated with a firm realizing headcount or contractor-cost reductions.

historical_analogs.value: The relevant analog is adoption of digital production tools in a creative workflow: individual experimentation precedes common team standards, client acceptance, and reliable commercial delivery, so use grows faster than captured labor savings.
historical_analogs.rationale: The sources describe bottom-up, largely self-taught AI adoption and collaboration friction, supporting a staged workflow-adoption analogy rather than a quantified historical industrial-design time series.
historical_analogs.plausible_range: Individual use can spread within 1-2 years; integrated labor savings can take 2-6 years
caveats: This is a qualitative analogy, not a measured history of CAD, rendering, or industrial-design employment.
Tool quality may advance faster than client approval, organizational policy, or product-liability acceptance.

owners_60plus_pct.rationale: No reviewed source measured owners over 60 for US 541420 firms or the supplied EBITDA band. The 25% selection is a deliberately broad planning assumption, not an observed demographic statistic.
caveats: Founder age and ownership are not inferable from professional-designer demographics or general small-business surveys.
This input needs a code- and size-specific owner survey before it can support acquisition sourcing claims.

succession_shortage_documented.rationale: No reviewed source documented a 541420 succession shortage. The stricter selection is false rather than treating founder branding or a general demographic assumption as proof.
succession_shortage_documented.plausible_range: true/false; selected false pending direct evidence
caveats: A shortage may exist locally, but it was not evidenced for this score input.

active_consolidators.rationale: One recent, directly reviewed capability acquisition is selected as a verified floor: Fen Technology acquired Wright Design in 2024. It demonstrates a strategic buyer for integrated product-development capability, not an active US PE roll-up market.
caveats: S6 is a UK transaction and not a count of US 541420 buyers.
Historic McKinsey-LUNAR is recorded as market precedent but is too old to increase the active-buyer count.

buy_mult.rationale: A 5.5x entry estimate is deliberately below broad surveyed-bankers' 6.8x typical middle-market expectation because a founder-led industrial-design studio has high client and key-person concentration, weak evidence of recurring revenue, and no direct comp database in the reviewed evidence.
caveats: S7 is broad middle-market M&A reporting, not an industrial-design or sub-$10M EBITDA transaction set.
Some studios may transact on adjusted EBITDA, revenue, acqui-hire, earnout, or talent-retention terms that are poorly represented by a simple EBITDA multiple.

exit_mult.rationale: A modest exit uplift assumes an acquirer has added engineering/prototype breadth, diversified customers, and reduced founder dependence. The selected multiple remains below broad typical middle-market expectations because reviewed design transactions are capability acquisitions with undisclosed terms, not comparable roll-up exits.
caveats: S5 is a 2015 acquisition and S6 is a 2024 UK transaction; neither discloses a usable multiple.
Multiple expansion is confined to this input and is not used to justify capture or terminal demand.

pricing_structure.value: Mix of defined fixed-price phases, exploratory fee-for-service or staged work, and occasional fee-plus-equity or royalty arrangements; the next scope definition, milestone, or renewal is the primary repricing point for AI productivity.
pricing_structure.rationale: The described product-design pricing models directly distinguish fixed defined work from exploratory hourly or staged work, which creates different timing for retention versus pass-through of AI savings.
pricing_structure.plausible_range: Project-specific mix from mostly fixed defined phases to mostly staged exploratory fee-for-service work
caveats: S3 is a single firm's commercial explanation in Canada and may not represent US lower-middle-market 541420 studios.
Pricing differs materially by product complexity, IP ownership, client procurement rules, and whether engineering/prototyping is bundled.

cross_checks.uplift_vs_A_consistent: The task assessment gives substantial credit to visual and documentation work, but adoption remains below 50% and the timing selection allows for the lag from individual use to integrated studio workflows. It does not imply immediate autonomous product development.

terminal_value.justification: External demand persists for differentiated research, engineering, prototypes, and product-development execution, but commoditized concepts and visual production face direct AI substitution and can be internalized by clients without a reviewed regulatory gate.

cross_checks.no_proxy_confirmed: No external values replaced the supplied labor share, role weights, total firms, or target-band count. Unobserved inputs are marked estimates, and broader, foreign, historical, or firm-specific evidence is disclosed rather than portrayed as a US 541420 measurement.
cross_checks.pricing_model_deflation: Fixed-scope retention and repricing pressure are scored in pi_dist and pi_moat. External-demand risk from client insourcing and AI-enabled concept production is scored only in terminal demand, not double-counted as a labor replacement or multiple claim.

top_uncertain_inputs.score_impact: Fast tool experimentation may not become durable workflow savings at the same pace, so the adoption factor is highly uncertain.
The 4-digit role contamination and large residual make the labor-value factor especially sensitive to a code-specific occupation mix.
Founder dependence, client insourcing, and whether the firm owns physical product-development capability determine retained savings.
No reviewed direct comp set supports conventional multiple expansion for standalone industrial-design studios.

evidence.reviewer_note: Research was conducted only for 541420. The packet deliberately treats role mix, labor share, ownership age, succession, pricing, and multiple support as weak where code-specific evidence was not found; no claim of a PE roll-up market is made.

research_memo.executive_view: Industrial design services present high workflow exposure but weak ownership of the resulting value. Generative tools can compress visual exploration, concept communication, research synthesis, presentation material, and administrative production; the defensible residue is user understanding, design direction, engineering judgment, prototype iteration, and client trust. The supplied labor share and occupation mix are unusually unreliable for this narrow code, so the calculated labor opportunity should be treated as an upper-bound signal rather than a deal thesis. The more fundamental concern is that a corporate client can apply the same tools internally or re-bid a fixed-scope engagement [S1][S2].
research_memo.how_ai_changes_work: The best evidence points to AI as a rapidly adopted collaborator in industrial-design workflows rather than a finished autonomous product-development system. Interviews with 20 experienced industrial designers report bottom-up adoption driven by speed pressure, while also identifying loss of control over micro-decisions as a risk [S1]. The most exposed tasks are mood boards, variants, rendering, research summaries, proposals, schedules, and routine documentation. Physical prototypes, manufacturability trade-offs, field research, client workshops, and responsibility for a production-ready choice still require humans and cross-functional work; an IDSA-listed product-development consultancy explicitly combines research, industrial design, engineering, testing, and in-house prototypes [S4].
research_memo.value_capture: Engagement economics create a short-lived retention window, not a durable moat. A product-design consultancy describes fixed fees for defined, mostly known work but describes fee-for-service and staged billing as more common for exploratory outsourced physical-product work [S3]. A studio can initially finish a fixed-price milestone with fewer hours, but renewal, change orders, and the next procurement cycle expose the new productivity to re-scoping and price competition. The capture estimates therefore assume only partial retention and a low studio moat; they do not use the physical prototype residue to inflate capture.
research_memo.adoption_timing: Effective use is below 50% of the selected task share but is likely to reach that point faster than in a regulated or field-service industry. A Foundation Capital and Designer Fund survey of nearly 400 designers found grassroots adoption, with startups adopting AI twice as fast as large firms, while the industrial-design interviews also describe rapid workflow adoption [S1][S2]. Those are not a representative census of US lower-middle-market 541420 studios, so the selected current adoption and three-and-a-half-year midpoint remain cautious estimates. Organizational IP, client confidentiality, CAD interoperability, and quality control are likely to slow conversion from tool use to sustained labor displacement.
research_memo.consolidation_economics: This is not a demonstrated PE roll-up market. McKinsey acquired LUNAR in 2015 to add design execution to an advisory practice, and a 2024 UK transaction combined Fen Technology with Wright Design to integrate industrial and mechanical design into a broader product-development offer [S5][S6]. Those are capability acquisitions, not proof that stand-alone studios clear conventional multiple-arbitrage exits. General middle-market valuation data is materially broader than this industry, so the small selected multiple spread assumes only a modest improvement if a buyer reduces founder dependence and adds engineering or prototyping breadth [S7].
research_memo.terminal_demand: Terminal demand is PRESSURED rather than melting immediately. Complex products still require external help where a client lacks research, engineering, prototype, or manufacturing capability; Delve's described service stack illustrates that broader physical-product-development offering [S4]. But no reviewed evidence establishes a regulatory or licensing gate that forces customers to retain an outside industrial-design studio. AI makes visual and early-concept work easier to internalize, and mature clients can combine an in-house team with outside specialists. A buyer should underwrite only the differentiated physical and strategic residue, not broad outsourced concept-production volume.
research_memo.risks_and_uncertainty: The decisive uncertainty is market boundary. The supplied 4-digit role mix is dominated by interior and graphic designers from sibling codes and omits Commercial and Industrial Designers, while the residual must stand in for the actual core product-design and engineering labor. The supplied 0.9177 labor share is also flagged as implausibly high by its own vintage construction. There is no reviewed 541420 owner-age survey, succession-shortage evidence, US studio multiple database, or US outsourcing-versus-insourcing time series. These evidence gaps make this a low-confidence underwriting case even though AI exposure is directionally clear.

Capture and exit assumptions partly reuse client substitution, project-pricing pressure and weak-portability mechanisms; this may over-penalize C and M even though the low-retention and melting-demand directions remain useful.
review.summary: Publishable with caveats. Exact mechanics, identity, model routing, manifest digests, deterministic dataset injection, fresh finalization, memo rendering and arithmetic all pass. Every registered source is accessible: Adobe supports the 40.9% daily-use proxy but not representative effective adoption or t50; BLS and Upwork support the core task, pricing and demand-risk directions; the M&A and succession sources are broad proxies whose limits and estimate status are disclosed. The packet avoids the prior adoption-segment aggregation error, keeps t50 positive below the chosen proxy boundary, preserves the contaminated 4-digit role mix rather than mutating it, and presents a useful low-confidence kill screen. The capture/exit/terminal mechanisms overlap enough to warrant an additional visible double-counting caveat, but the reopened evidence does not show that this overlap alone is likely to reverse the thesis without also changing multiple independent low-retention and exit judgments.

ai_replaceable_share.rationale: Weighted exposure estimate based on software-centered graphic-design tasks and current creative-tool use. It preserves every immutable role share, discounts the contaminated Interior Designers row, and assigns a high but uncertain fraction to the unlisted residual because it likely contains junior production and adjacent design labor.
caveats: Role weights are supplied at 4-digit 5414, not 541430.
The 27-1025 Interior Designers row is known sibling-code contamination and is not treated as representative of the target code.
No task-frequency survey maps automation shares directly to these wage shares.

breakdown_by_role.rationale: Sibling-contaminated interior-design row; only limited visual-concept and rendering work is analogous to the target code, so this row is heavily discounted.
BLS identifies software-based layouts, logos, visual concepts, revisions and production as central duties; Adobe shows graphic designers lead listed creative roles in daily AI use [S1][S2].
Scheduling, proposals and reporting compress, while client ownership, hiring and commercial judgment remain human-accountable.

breakdown_by_role.role: Sales Representatives of Services, Except Advertising, Insurance, Financial Services, and Travel
breakdown_by_role.rationale: Prospecting material, qualification and CRM work automate partly; relationship selling and project scoping remain less substitutable.
Brief summaries, schedules, status reporting and revision routing are tool-friendly, although client escalation remains human.
General office coordination and document handling are highly automatable relative to creative-client work.

breakdown_by_role.role: Laborers and Freight, Stock, and Material Movers, Hand
breakdown_by_role.rationale: Physical moving and handling have little direct generative-AI substitution.

breakdown_by_role.role: Market Research Analysts and Marketing Specialists
breakdown_by_role.rationale: Research synthesis, basic audience analysis and first-draft marketing material are substantially compressible, while strategy and accountability remain.

breakdown_by_role.role: Secretaries and Administrative Assistants, Except Legal, Medical, and Executive
breakdown_by_role.rationale: Calendar, correspondence, intake and records work are highly automatable.
AI can accelerate concept generation and versioning, but senior visual judgment, approval and client leadership retain meaningful human work.
The material unlisted wage residual is uncertain; in this code it is conservatively treated as predominantly production-adjacent and junior design labor rather than as hidden protected IP work.

pi_dist.rationale: Portfolio reputation and local client relationships create some differentiation, but BLS describes widely accessible software-based work and Upwork shows comparable global project supply.
caveats: No code-level buyer survey measures differentiation.

pi_moat.rationale: Brand standards, client context and accountable senior review can protect a small share, but the principal output remains software-mediated visual production with increasing AI enablement.
caveats: This is a service moat judgment, not a claim of transferable proprietary technology.

t50_years.rationale: Adobe reports 40.9% daily AI use among surveyed graphic designers, below the 50% boundary. It does not provide a representative workflow-weighted 541430 rate, so I use a bounded two-year estimate to the effective-adoption threshold and do not combine daily-use and pragmatic-segment measures.
t50_years.plausible_range: 1–3 years; zero only with representative workflow-weighted evidence above 50%
caveats: Adobe's sample is creative professionals and other creatives across three countries, not a U.S. NAICS establishment census.
Daily use is not a workflow-weighted effective-adoption measure; the separately reported pragmatic segment is not combined with it.

caveats: The source does not publish a 541430-specific all-frequency adoption rate.
Daily use among surveyed graphic designers is a conservative proxy, not a representative workflow-weighted measure of effective adoption.

historical_analogs.value: Desktop publishing, templates and digital production software reduced the scarcity of routine layout work; generative AI extends that pattern to ideation and asset creation, while client review and senior accountability persist.
historical_analogs.rationale: BLS documents software-centric production and the AI contracting risk; the historical comparison is an analytical analogy rather than a measured causal estimate.
historical_analogs.plausible_range: Routine production compresses more than senior client-facing work
caveats: No code-specific historical price series was located.

owners_60plus_pct.rationale: A deliberately low-confidence 20% assumption for the target code; Gallup supplies only generic context that many small and nonemployer businesses lack transfer plans, not owner age by NAICS.
caveats: No 541430 owner-age distribution was found.

succession_shortage_documented.rationale: Generic small-business uncertainty is documented, but no evidence establishes a 541430-specific shortage of sellable firms or succession supply-demand imbalance.
succession_shortage_documented.plausible_range: false unless code-specific evidence is found
caveats: Do not convert generic closure and uncertainty data into a code-specific acquisition pipeline.

active_consolidators.rationale: One minimally confirmed strategic-acquirer case is counted from the broad creative-agency report; the available evidence does not demonstrate a deep, current 541430 lower-middle-market roll-up field.
caveats: The report's transaction set extends beyond pure graphic design and includes larger agencies.

buy_mult.rationale: A conservative illustrative entry multiple for a small, key-person-dependent service firm. The broad source says traditional service models command lower values than technology-driven agencies, but it cannot provide a code-specific LMM price.
caveats: Not a transaction-comp-derived 541430 multiple; no investment recommendation follows.

exit_mult.rationale: Illustrative exit multiple below entry reflects AI-driven product replacement risk, project pricing and limited portable moat; broad creative-agency M&A evidence does not offset those code-level risks.
caveats: This is an underwriting stress assumption, not an observed valuation statistic.

pricing_structure.value: Hourly and fixed-project fees, with rush premiums and scope/revision sensitivity
pricing_structure.rationale: Upwork describes hourly rates and common fixed pricing for graphic-design work, including scope, expertise and timeline effects.
pricing_structure.plausible_range: Project and hourly pricing; retainers may occur but are not established as dominant
caveats: Marketplace historical contracts are not a census of U.S. agency billing.

cross_checks.uplift_vs_A_consistent: Yes. The role-weighted exposure estimate is high, while the conservative 40.9% daily-use proxy remains below the 50% boundary; the selected two-year t50 yields A=5 and does not assert that all work or revenue disappears immediately.

terminal_value.justification: The central deliverable is replicable visual-production output. Senior brand governance and client accountability survive, but they are too narrow and key-person-dependent to establish durable code-wide terminal demand.

cross_checks.no_proxy_confirmed: No invented fallback was used for immutable dataset fields. The supplied 4-digit 5414 role mix is itself a disclosed proxy for 541430 and includes known 541410 sibling contamination; it is preserved rather than reweighted.
cross_checks.pricing_model_deflation: Hourly and fixed-project pricing exposes production hours to automation, template substitution and worldwide freelance comparison. Faster generation can lower revenue per project even when a human remains responsible for the approved output.

top_uncertain_inputs.plausible_range: 1–3 years; zero requires representative workflow-weighted evidence that effective adoption is already above 50%
top_uncertain_inputs.score_impact: At the selected two years A is 5; it would be 10 only at zero and falls to 10/t50 when the threshold has not yet been crossed.
The contaminated role mix and large residual could move V materially, although V is already capped at 10 under the selected estimate.
Exit at or below entry produces M=0; evidence is not adequate for a positive-arbitrage underwriting claim.

evidence.reviewer_note: Attempt-2 remediation of the prior material adoption finding. The Adobe daily-use rate is retained as a conservative below-threshold proxy; it is not combined with the separate pragmatic-segment statistic. This remains a kill-screen under explicit product-replacement risk, not a claim that all graphic-design demand ends. Treat the broad creative-agency M&A evidence and generic succession evidence as contextual only.

research_memo.executive_view: Graphic Design Services is a high-AI-exposure, poor-roll-up screen rather than a consolidation thesis. The frozen labor model is broad and materially contaminated by sibling interior-design employment, but even a conservative reading leaves much of the actual layout, image, copy-adjacent and production workflow exposed. Graphic designers themselves report the highest daily AI use among the creative roles in Adobe's June 2026 study (40.9%), and BLS explicitly says automated design tools may reduce demand for freelance designers [S1][S2].
research_memo.how_ai_changes_work: Generative tools compress briefing, moodboarding, concept variants, image manipulation, layout iteration and production adaptation. BLS describes the core work as creating visual concepts, layouts, logos and final production using software, with client revisions and error review [S2]. The surviving work is more likely to be client interpretation, choice among alternatives, brand-system stewardship, approval management and accountability; it is not a reason to assume the original production hours remain billable.
research_memo.value_capture: There is a narrow service layer around trusted client relationships, proprietary brand systems and senior art direction, but it is weakly portable in the small-firm population. Upwork's marketplace evidence shows worldwide graphic-design rates around $15–$35 per hour and frequent fixed-price work, indicating transparent comparison and price pressure rather than a protected, recurring asset base [S3]. A portfolio and client trust can matter, but neither is a durable code-wide moat when customers can generate a first draft or source freelancers globally.
research_memo.adoption_timing: The available evidence does not establish that effective workflow adoption has crossed 50%, so I set t50 to two years rather than zero under the frozen rule. Adobe reports 40.9% daily AI use among surveyed graphic designers, which I use only as a conservative current-adoption proxy; I do not add it to Adobe's separately described mostly-weekly pragmatic segment [S1]. The evidence is neither a 541430 establishment census nor workflow-weighted, so adoption could be slower or faster by task and buyer; the product-replacement thesis does not depend on asserting a threshold already crossed.
research_memo.consolidation_economics: The supplied band contains 370 firms but the underlying firm distribution is overwhelmingly below five employees. That makes founder/key-person dependence, customer concentration and individual portfolios central diligence risks. The available independent-creative-agency transaction report is a broad, upper-market comparator, not a 541430 lower-middle-market comp; it says traditional service models command lower values than technology-driven agencies and describes selective strategic acquisitions [S4]. I therefore use one minimally confirmed strategic consolidator and set buy/exit assumptions conservatively rather than importing the report's broad-sector headline multiples.
research_memo.terminal_demand: Terminal demand is MELTING. Clients will still need decisions, brand governance, high-stakes launches and accountable creative leadership, but the code's core deliverable—layouts, logos, graphics and production assets—is increasingly obtainable through software, templates or global freelancers. BLS's projection explicitly notes that automated design tools such as AI may reduce companies' need to contract with freelance graphic designers [S2]. This is product replacement risk, not merely a temporary labor-efficiency cycle.
research_memo.risks_and_uncertainty: The score is intentionally fragile. The immutable role mix is at 4-digit 5414 and its largest listed occupation, Interior Designers, is identified in the brief as sibling-code contamination; I do not reweight it. Adobe is a multi-country survey of creatives rather than a NAICS revenue study, marketplace rates are not audited firm economics, and the M&A report covers independent creative agencies rather than small pure-play graphic-design firms [S1][S3][S4]. These limitations lower confidence and favor a kill rather than a precision valuation claim.

review.summary: Mechanics and exact artifact bindings pass. The reopened sources support the stated fashion-workflow, adoption, pricing, succession, and M&A-boundary facts; the record remains useful because every material code-scope, role-mix, marketplace, adoption, and transaction-evidence limitation is explicitly disclosed and the resulting kill thesis does not depend on hiding those proxies.

cross_checks.uplift_vs_A_consistent: Yes. High task exposure and adoption at the 50% boundary refer to digital workflow tasks; they do not imply immediate replacement of fitting, fabrication, sourcing or factory-facing work.

terminal_value.justification: The hypothesized dominant fashion-design service retains paid demand where it turns a concept into a manufacturable product, but its visual ideation and documentation layers are exposed. Physical-fabrication niches are more durable and pure design-fee niches weaker.

cross_checks.no_proxy_confirmed: No fallback was invented for immutable dataset fields. The required 4-digit 5414 role mix is a material proxy for 541490 and its leading Interior and Graphic Designer rows are sibling-code contamination; the record preserves and discounts rather than replaces them.
cross_checks.pricing_model_deflation: Project and milestone packages invite clients to request the same deliverable for less when AI reduces drafts, variants and documentation time. Physical samples, fit sessions and factory handoff can preserve work but do not necessarily preserve the lost design-hour revenue.

Exit at or below entry produces M=0; no direct 541490 transaction set supports an expansion thesis.

evidence.reviewer_note: Fresh live sources were opened on 2026-07-21. The fashion-design thesis is a cautiously selected dominant-subsegment hypothesis, not a code-level revenue fact. The record deliberately excludes fashion-brand M&A as a valuation comparable for independent specialized-design services.

research_memo.executive_view: Other Specialized Design Services is too heterogeneous to underwrite as one clean roll-up. I score fashion and accessory design as the hypothesized dominant fee subsegment, while treating jewelry, textile, footwear, costume and fabrication-heavy exhibit/sample work as a heterogeneous tail. In that dominant workflow, AI already crosses the timing threshold but does not eliminate the physical handoff to materials, fit and production. The deal screen fails because the available evidence does not establish transferable fee books or a code-specific buyer universe, not because every specialized-design task is instantly automated.
research_memo.how_ai_changes_work: Fashion-design work mixes idea generation with production reality. BLS lists trend study, collection themes, CAD design, fabric selection, prototypes, presentations and final-production oversight as normal duties [S2]. Marketplace listings similarly bundle mood boards, flats, tech packs and sourcing with manufacturer collaboration [S4]. Generative AI and design software can accelerate sketches, variants, mood boards, early visualizations and documentation; physical sampling, fit correction, material choice, factory communication and approval retain a human-and-physical loop.
research_memo.value_capture: Captured savings should be modest. The external market exposes comparable freelancers worldwide and commonly packages a garment, tech pack, collection or launch as a defined project; Upwork lists fashion-design work at roughly $20–$50 per hour and discrete project budgets [S4]. This supports some fee retention for named taste, client context and production know-how, but clients can re-scope work when first-draft visual and technical material becomes cheaper. The savings channel is distinct from terminal demand: a studio may keep a fabrication or sourcing engagement while losing design hours and price per deliverable.
research_memo.adoption_timing: I set current effective adoption to 51.7% and t50 to zero. The direct source is not a 541490 census: SNAAP's Fall 2025 U.S. survey of more than 2,000 arts-and-design alumni found 51.7% used generative AI professionally, with productivity, ideation and editing as common motivations [S1]. Fashion-specific executive evidence is directionally consistent but lower and broader: more than 35% of fashion and luxury executives report using generative AI in selected functions [S3]. The threshold conclusion is therefore low-confidence and applies to workflow use, not to full physical-product substitution.
research_memo.consolidation_economics: The supplied target band contains only 82 firms and the total establishment population is heavily micro-firmed, making named-designer, client and factory relationships central portability risks. Broad fashion M&A evidence is not a substitute: current transactions center on brands, retail scale, licensing and portfolios rather than independent specialized-design-service studios [S6]. I count no verified active consolidators for this target and use stressed illustrative entry/exit assumptions. Generic small-business succession evidence can support a broad owner-age proxy, but it cannot create a code-specific supply pipeline [S5].
research_memo.terminal_demand: Terminal demand is PRESSURED rather than MELTING. In the dominant fashion-design hypothesis, paid external work survives where a studio converts a concept into a manufacturable garment: selection of fabrics and trims, prototype and fit feedback, sourcing, production files and the client/factory relationship matter [S2][S4]. But pure visual concepts, pattern/color variants and first-draft specifications are directly exposed to AI-enabled in-house teams and global freelancers. Fabrication-heavy and sample-room niches are more durable; pure sketch, textile-motif and fee-only design niches are more vulnerable. That divergence is why the code remains heterogeneous.
research_memo.risks_and_uncertainty: The largest uncertainty is classification, not arithmetic. The immutable role mix is a 4-digit 5414 cell whose leading Interior and Graphic Designer rows belong principally to sibling codes; the likely core Fashion Designers and physical sample roles are in the unlisted residual. Fashion is only a hypothesis for the dominant revenue subsegment, and the research sources describe occupations, creative alumni, fashion brands or marketplace work rather than U.S. 541490 revenue. I therefore preserve the supplied shares, disclose the proxy, discount the contaminated rows, and treat both consolidation and valuation inputs as low-confidence stress assumptions.

ai_replaceable_share.rationale: AI coding use and productivity evidence supports substantial task compression in code, tests and documentation, while trust, review and client-accountability limits keep each role fraction below full automation.
ai_replaceable_share.plausible_range: 0.26-0.43 of total wage bill after role weighting
caveats: The sources survey developers or development organizations, not occupations within US NAICS 541511.
The supplied role mix is four-digit 5415 and understates the code-delivery concentration of a pure custom-programming shop.
This input measures technical task replaceability only; it does not take credit for adoption speed, client demand or pricing.

breakdown_by_role.rationale: Generation, refactoring, tests and documentation are exposed, but architecture, review, debugging in client context and production accountability remain.
Tier-one questions and routine troubleshooting are highly amenable to assistants, while escalations and client empathy persist.
Status, allocation and reporting compress, but delivery leadership, client escalation and risk ownership remain human-accountable.
Documentation and process analysis are assisted, while eliciting ambiguous requirements and organizational navigation remain.

breakdown_by_role.role: Sales Representatives of Services, Except Advertising, Insurance, Financial Services, and Travel

This mixed computer-occupations category includes technical implementation work exposed to AI, tempered by specialized administration and security work.
Scheduling, status and documentation compress, while scope trade-offs, stakeholder alignment and escalation remain.
Research, analysis drafts and process mapping are assisted, but client diagnosis and change management remain.

breakdown_by_role.rationale: Test generation and routine QA are directly exposed, with test strategy, release risk and acceptance judgment persisting.
The unlisted wage share likely mixes additional billable developers with administration; a mid-range fraction avoids treating it as either all code or all back office.

pi_dist.rationale: Effort-based pricing is transitioning toward outcome, experience and subscription structures, but the latter are emerging models and the typical small custom-programming engagement can still pass efficiency to the client through fewer hours or lower FTE need.
caveats: ISG covers broader application-development outsourcing and Globant is a scaled provider, not a US lower-middle-market 541511 sample.
No fresh source measures the T&M, staff-augmentation or fixed-price mix for the target EBITDA band.

pi_moat.rationale: Broader AI-driven ADM contracts can add governance, remediation and operational accountability, which creates some client-specific retention; a custom-development project's code, documentation and people can nevertheless be transferred, so this is a modest moat rather than a durable license-like one.
caveats: The source includes managed services and global systems integrators, which often have stickier contracts than a project-oriented 541511 firm.
This is a capture input only and does not use human accountability to claim a terminal-demand floor.

t50_years.rationale: The selected current effective adoption estimate is already above 50%, so the frozen rule sets t50 to zero rather than forecasting additional years.
t50_years.plausible_range: 0 years if effective adoption is at least 50%; otherwise 1-3 years
caveats: Tool use and self-reported productivity are not identical to effective adoption across all billable tasks.
The evidence is not a small-firm, NAICS-specific longitudinal adoption measure.

current_adoption_pct.rationale: A deliberately discounted effective-use estimate remains above 50% given GitHub's high enterprise tool-use figures, DORA's reported productivity effects and current Stack Overflow evidence, while recognizing that not every user applies AI across meaningful client delivery.
caveats: GitHub's survey was 500 US developers at companies with 1,000+ employees and predates the current tool generation.
Stack Overflow and DORA do not isolate US lower-middle-market custom-programming firms.

historical_analogs.value: AI coding tools extend earlier developer automation by moving from assistance into generation, testing and documentation; the current change is broad but still constrained by trust, review and deployment accountability.
historical_analogs.rationale: Developer surveys and DORA evidence show workflow efficiency layered into ongoing engineering rather than a fully autonomous delivery process.
historical_analogs.plausible_range: Incremental workflow compression to material role redesign
caveats: This is qualitative context only and is not used to set adoption timing beyond the separate adoption inputs.

owners_60plus_pct.rationale: No fresh, code-specific owner-age statistic was found for US 541511, so a conservative nonzero estimate is used without claiming a sector-54 succession pattern.
caveats: This is not a measured owner-age rate and should not be read as evidence of a succession wave.

succession_shortage_documented.rationale: No documentation of a 541511-specific succession shortage was found in the fresh research pass, so no shortage credit is taken.
succession_shortage_documented.plausible_range: true/false; selected false absent direct documentation
caveats: Absence of a located source is not evidence that no individual firm faces succession risk.

active_consolidators.rationale: A bounded count of plausible strategic and PE-backed technology-services buyers is used: Accenture reports sustained acquisition activity, and the IT-services sources describe strategic and PE-backed consolidation. It is intentionally not a claim that eight buyers are bidding for every small 541511 target.
caveats: The evidence covers broad IT services, including MSPs, cloud and systems integration rather than a deal tally for 541511.
This factor is used only for buyability and does not support adoption, capture or terminal demand.

buy_mult.rationale: Alinea's 10.8x average includes premiums for strategic assets and cloud capability and is heavily influenced by MSP demand for recurring revenue. A generic custom-programming/staff-augmentation operator therefore receives a below-benchmark 7.5x entry estimate.
caveats: This is a UK IT-services benchmark, not disclosed US lower-middle-market 541511 transactions.
Contractor pass-through and owner compensation normalization can materially change an apparent EBITDA multiple.

exit_mult.rationale: A 9.5x exit remains below Alinea's broad 10.8x IT-services average and assumes the buyer has converted some delivery into repeatable, outcome-linked contracts; ISG identifies that commercial direction but does not demonstrate it for every small firm.
caveats: No direct evidence supports multiple expansion for a generic 541511 firm; the selected exit is conditional on contract and revenue-quality improvement.
The source mix includes managed services and cloud assets with more recurring revenue than custom programming.

pricing_structure.value: Historically effort-based time-and-materials, fixed-price and staff-augmentation engagements are transitioning selectively toward outcome-, experience- and subscription-linked structures.
pricing_structure.rationale: ISG explicitly describes conventional T&M/fixed-price contracting giving way to outcome and experience agreements, while Globant's AI Pods give a concrete subscription example.
pricing_structure.plausible_range: Predominantly effort-based to meaningfully outcome/subscription-linked depending on client and provider
caveats: No source quantifies the current pricing-model mix for US $1-10M EBITDA 541511 operators.

cross_checks.uplift_vs_A_consistent: The task-replaceability estimate and current adoption are distinct: role fractions describe technically compressible work, while adoption is discounted for effective use. A zero t50 follows only from the selected adoption level, not from the large target pool or consolidation evidence.

terminal_value.justification: Integration, security, governance and production accountability sustain paid work, but AI attacks the scarce coding labor and effort-priced revenue base. Outcome pricing may preserve selected operators' economics; it does not establish durable demand for generic external custom programming.

cross_checks.no_proxy_confirmed: No broad IT-services or global-provider source is presented as a direct NAICS 541511 small-firm measurement. Each such scope mismatch is stated in the relevant caveats and market-boundary disclosure.
cross_checks.pricing_model_deflation: Pricing-model deflation is modeled in pi_dist: fewer billed hours and smaller staff-augmentation needs pass AI productivity to clients unless a firm actually shifts to outcome, subscription or reusable-product economics. It is not counted again as task replaceability.

top_uncertain_inputs.plausible_range: 0 years if the discounted effective-adoption estimate is at least 50%; 1-3 years if tool use has not translated into meaningful billable delivery.
top_uncertain_inputs.score_impact: Crossing below 50% would replace the zero-year timing score with a materially lower score.
Capture changes linearly with distribution; widespread T&M/staff-augmentation repricing would keep it near the low end.
The modest exit uplift is conditional on revenue-quality improvement; a flat services multiple would reduce the multiple factor materially.
No direct owner-age source exists in this record, so buyability can move meaningfully with actual founder demographics.

evidence.reviewer_note: Treat the commercial-model transition as the underwriting question. A target with recurring, accountable application work and authority to price outcomes may retain AI savings; an interchangeable FTE/staff-augmentation book may instead experience revenue deflation and should not receive the selected exit case without evidence.

research_memo.executive_view: Custom programming has a large, directly exposed delivery workforce and AI coding tools are already common among enterprise developers. The buy-side opportunity is not simply cheaper labor: it is to acquire a client-context-rich firm at a services multiple and convert selected work to repeatable, outcome-linked delivery. That is a demanding transformation, not the base case for a generic staff-augmentation book.
research_memo.how_ai_changes_work: Code generation, tests, documentation and routine defect work are the most exposed portions of delivery. GitHub's enterprise-developer survey found widespread tool use, while DORA links use and trust to reported productivity. The same evidence leaves review, security, architecture, requirements elicitation, legacy-system interpretation and production accountability in the human workflow; this supports task compression rather than a claim that software teams disappear.
research_memo.value_capture: The primary capture risk is commercial. ISG describes application-development outsourcing moving from effort-based contracts toward outcome, experience and subscription structures, and Globant has introduced a metered subscription model for AI-powered engineering. Those are credible routes to retain savings, but they are evidence of a transition led by scaled providers, not proof that a lower-middle-market 541511 firm has escaped hourly or FTE-rate repricing. The selected distribution and moat inputs therefore remain low.
research_memo.adoption_timing: Effective use is judged already above the 50% boundary, so the timing input is zero years by rule. This is supported directionally by GitHub's 2023 enterprise survey, the 2025 Stack Overflow survey and DORA's productivity findings, but it is not a measured adoption rate for small US programming shops. Governance, accuracy concerns and review discipline can still make realized savings uneven across client codebases.
research_memo.consolidation_economics: The target band is unusually large, and strategic and PE-backed buyers are active across technology services. Accenture reported 23 strategic acquisitions in fiscal 2025, while Alinea tracked 110 IT-services deals in the twelve months through Q1 2025. Alinea's 10.8x average is materially influenced by recurring-revenue MSP and cloud assets, so the selected buy and exit multiples remain below that benchmark for a generic custom-development firm and assume only a modest improvement in repeatability.
research_memo.terminal_demand: Terminal demand is PRESSURED, not melting. Enterprises will still need accountable partners for integration, modernization, security, operational continuity and regulated production change, and ISG reports contracts adding governance, remediation and performance accountability. But AI directly lowers the scarce labor input that made outsourced custom programming valuable; if clients keep work in-house or continue to buy hours, the paid external demand and its price can shrink even as delivery becomes more efficient.
research_memo.risks_and_uncertainty: There is no fresh, code-specific owner-age or succession-shortage statistic, so ownership buyability is deliberately low-confidence and no shortage is credited. Sources blend 541511 with application-development outsourcing, managed services, systems integration and global providers; the record marks those boundary mismatches rather than treating them as direct US lower-middle-market observations. Contractor pass-through, bench utilization, client concentration and the share of staff augmentation versus fixed-price work can each change both margins and AI savings capture materially.
