evidence.reviewer_note: Reopened every listed score-driving source on 2026-07-21. Underwrite exact NAICS scope, net revenue, client concentration, owner normalization, contract durability and fee structure at target level.

research_memo.executive_view: 541810 covers businesses that create campaigns and place advertising, offering creative, account-management, production, media-planning and buying capabilities [S1]. This makes it highly AI-exposed, but that exposure does not create an easy roll-up: labor-based fees and procurement can pass cost savings to clients, while in-housing and platform automation challenge the product itself. The screen therefore gives meaningful task automation but low retained capture and a conservative small-agency multiple bridge.
research_memo.how_ai_changes_work: Generative tools can reduce first-draft copy, asset versions, production design, research synthesis, media plans, reporting, billing support and project coordination. Client judgment, brand accountability, senior selling and relationship continuity remain human-intensive. The supplied 5418 role mix is a four-digit proxy contaminated by media representatives, display and promotional occupations; judgments explicitly discount those lines and use a bounded residual for account service, production and administrative roles absent from the top ten [S1].
research_memo.value_capture: ANA reports that labor-based fees remained the dominant agency-compensation form, especially among smaller advertisers, even as fixed/output fees gained use [S3]. Under labor-based scopes, AI hours saved invite procurement repricing rather than pure agency margin retention. Output fees can retain a spread, but that benefit is offset by platforms and in-house teams becoming cheaper substitutes. This is why pi_dist is low and separate from the still-real value of a strong client relationship or specialist positioning.
research_memo.adoption_timing: BTOS evidence reports 18% of firms using AI in a business function in late 2025/early 2026, with high use in knowledge-intensive professional services but limited functional breadth among adopters [S2]. Advertising's task profile should progress faster than the economy-wide average, but effective adoption includes workflow, approvals, brand risk and client billing, not merely tool use. Three years to 50% effective operational adoption is a judgment rather than a claim that 541810 already has 50% adoption.
research_memo.consolidation_economics: The frozen dataset estimates 1,175 firms in the $1–10M EBITDA band, largely 20–99-employee agencies. JEGI reports that digital-marketing assets with £4m–£7.5m EBITDA traded at a 10.8x median, while larger assets received still higher multiples [S5]. That supports real deal interest but is neither a U.S. 541810 series nor a comp for $1–4M EBITDA agencies. A 5.5x entry and 7.0x exit reflect a deliberate discount for smaller scale, client concentration, owner dependence and normalized-EBITDA uncertainty.
research_memo.terminal_demand: The paid product is pressured, not automatically melting. Agencies retain value where they provide brand judgment, accountable coordination, specialist vertical knowledge and integrated execution; those services sit within the exact NAICS definition [S1]. But ANA reports 82% of members had an in-house agency in 2023 and the cited research reports meaningful internal cost and time advantages [S4]. AI-native creative and platform automation therefore put durable pressure on undifferentiated production and media execution.
research_memo.risks_and_uncertainty: The key risks are client concentration, principal-led revenue, labor-fee repricing, in-housing and boundary leakage into PR, media buying, marketing consulting and design. The available multiple evidence covers digital marketing and larger assets, not exact NAICS 541810 lower-middle-market transactions [S5]. The target screen should be underwritten on net revenue rather than gross media billings, normalized owner compensation, churn and contracts; no generic agency statistic substitutes for that diligence.

evidence.reviewer_note: Initial fleet packet: all cited score-driving URLs were reopened on 2026-07-21; estimates carry explicit source/proxy caveats.

research_memo.executive_view: Public Relations Agencies remain a real lower-middle-market acquisition universe, but the investable product is narrowing. The official NAICS definition covers campaigns, lobbying, political consulting and PR consulting [S1]; those services share a need for senior judgment and trust, while the production layer is rapidly assisted by AI. A buyer can harvest meaningful internal efficiency, but should underwrite a pressured terminal demand profile rather than a broad, durable retainer book.
research_memo.how_ai_changes_work: AI is compressing drafting, monitoring, research, media-list preparation, repurposing and reporting, not the whole client outcome. Muck Rack survey reporting says 75% of PR professionals had incorporated AI into their work by 2025 [S2], while USC documents agencies and corporate teams using AI to streamline daily work and appointing AI leadership [S3]. Crisis counsel, executive access, relationship management and accountable advice remain human-intensive, so the role estimate does not treat the profession as fully replaceable.
research_memo.value_capture: AI savings initially repair unbilled over-service and improve account-team utilization. Retainer pricing can let an agency retain part of that saving, but clients see staffing and deliverables and can reset scope at renewal. The selected distribution and moat fractions are deliberately moderate: media relationships and specialization protect a portion of price, whereas generic release, content and reporting work faces visible deflation. This is a capture judgment, not a claim that retained hours establish terminal demand.
research_memo.adoption_timing: The 75% professional-use survey measure is above the scorecard's 50% effective-adoption threshold, so t50 is zero by the frozen rule [S2]. It is not a NAICS 541820 establishment census and does not prove every workflow is automated. The high usage reading nevertheless makes waiting for a distant adoption inflection implausible; implementation quality and client policy, rather than access to tools, are the limiting variables.
research_memo.consolidation_economics: PRovoke tracked 80 PR-sector deals in 2025 and reported a tilt toward larger, integrated sellers; it also identifies PE-backed buyers and data/technology capability as a value driver [S5]. That supports active strategic interest but is not proof that a $1-10M EBITDA generalist agency has the same demand. Founder-linked books remain central: a PR-agency transaction guide describes 60-70% cash at close and 30-40% earnouts tied to client and senior-publicist retention [S4], making headline consideration a weak proxy for immediately transferable EBITDA.
research_memo.terminal_demand: Paid demand should survive in specialist, regulated, crisis and high-stakes corporate communications, but the broad generalist product is pressured. Muck Rack's survey reporting highlights media relations as the leading job function and respondents cite low journalist response rates and shrinking newsrooms as obstacles [S2]. That is a non-AI headwind layered on AI's removal of routine production. The likely endpoint is fewer paid senior-counsel hours per account, not simply the same retainer delivered at a lower cost.
research_memo.risks_and_uncertainty: The deterministic role mix is a 4-digit 5418 proxy that includes advertising and media-representation occupations, so the role model uses a large explicitly uncertain residual. No NAICS-specific owner-age statistic or private lower-middle-market PR multiple database was available in the reopened sources. The multiple is a broad marketing-agency proxy and the ownership, consolidator and capture estimates should be diligence items, especially client concentration, founder dependence, earnout mechanics, renewal language and specialty mix.

t50_years.rationale: The 68% agency-side display-programmatic measure exceeds 50%, requiring zero under the frozen threshold rule.
caveats: This is a Europe-wide display automation proxy, not a US NAICS-specific AI adoption measure.

current_adoption_pct.rationale: IAB Europe reports 68% agency-side display budgets purchased programmatically in 2024; it is used as the effective automation baseline.
caveats: The observed measure is European and display-specific; it is not a US 541830 establishment statistic or a pure generative-AI measure.

historical_analogs.value: Programmatic buying already shifted execution from manual placement to software-mediated buying; generative and agentic AI extend automation from execution into campaign decisions and management.
historical_analogs.rationale: The sources directly describe programmatic agency use and AI moving into campaign operations.
historical_analogs.plausible_range: Operational compression with strategic-service retention
caveats: Analogy does not predict target-level revenue retention.

owners_60plus_pct.rationale: The consolidation evidence supports a fragmented-independent landscape but does not measure owner age; selected value is a cautious operating assumption.
caveats: No NAICS-specific owner-age study was found in reopened sources.

succession_shortage_documented.rationale: No reopened source documents a NAICS-specific succession shortage.
caveats: This is not proof that no individual target has a succession issue.

active_consolidators.rationale: The Empower-Ocean transaction and named Horizon benchmark demonstrate at least several active scaled independent-media buyers; count is conservative rather than a market census.
caveats: Large-scale buyer activity does not prove appetite for every small target.

buy_mult.rationale: A lower-middle-market fee-EBITDA purchase uses the bottom of the guide's 4.5-6x well-run range to reflect transparency and client-concentration risk.
caveats: S6 is commercial guidance rather than a disclosed transaction database; EBITDA must be on net fee revenue, not gross billings.

exit_mult.rationale: A 0.5x improvement assumes documented client retention, transferable vendor economics and data capability, not generic multiple expansion.
caveats: Exit value depends on normalization of pass-through and principal/rebate economics.

pricing_structure.value: Management fees, commissions or percentage-of-spend arrangements; all underwriting should normalize gross billings to net fee revenue.
pricing_structure.rationale: Official scope is buying/resale and the valuation guide states buyers value net fee revenue rather than pass-through media spend.
pricing_structure.plausible_range: Fee/commission-heavy with target-specific principal economics
caveats: Reported receipts and EBITDA can be distorted by gross pass-through and rebates.

cross_checks.uplift_vs_A_consistent: Automation is already broad, creating immediate operating-efficiency availability; the score does not treat that as evidence that fees or dollars managed will endure.

terminal_value.justification: Cross-platform advisory remains paid, but programmatic and AI automate core execution while platforms and transparency pressure erode the generalist intermediary position [S2][S3][S4].

cross_checks.no_proxy_confirmed: No; the role mix is 4-digit 5418 and the adoption measure is a Europe-wide programmatic proxy, both disclosed.
cross_checks.pricing_model_deflation: Commission/fee structures can retain cost savings initially, but transparency reviews, fee audits, self-serve platforms and gross-to-net normalization create deflation and margin-reversal risk.

top_uncertain_inputs.plausible_range: 0 under the programmatic proxy threshold; US firm-level AI adoption is not directly observed
top_uncertain_inputs.score_impact: A remains 10 at zero; a stricter AI-only interpretation would lower A.
Moves V materially because the residual is large.
Moves M; gross-billing multiples are invalid for the target.

evidence.reviewer_note: Initial fleet packet: all cited score-driving URLs were reopened on 2026-07-21; valuation and adoption proxies are explicitly disclosed.

research_memo.executive_view: Media Buying Agencies have an unusually clear AI efficiency story but a fragile lower-middle-market terminal-demand story. NAICS 541830 is specifically the purchase of advertising time or space from media outlets for resale [S1], not creative advertising or selling-side representation. Platform automation and programmatic execution make execution cheaper, yet those same tools shift bargaining power toward platforms and in-house teams. This is a conditional, diligence-heavy acquisition thesis rather than a straightforward AI roll-up.
research_memo.how_ai_changes_work: Planning, audience research, trafficking, pacing, optimization, reconciliation and reporting are increasingly automated. The IAB Europe survey reports that agencies bought more than half of display budgets programmatically in 2024, with the agency figure at 68% [S2]. IAB's US revenue report also describes AI moving from automation toward models that make autonomous campaign decisions and manage campaigns end to end [S3]. Senior negotiation, cross-platform measurement, vendor relationships and client strategy remain important but are a smaller operational moat than routine execution.
research_memo.value_capture: Commission or percentage-of-spend pricing can preserve labor savings better than an hourly-service model, but it also exposes the operator to fee-audit, transparency and self-service pressure. ANA's K2 study found pervasive non-transparent practices and cash rebates across the US media-buying ecosystem [S4]; that supports the need to normalize profit and not treat opaque principal economics as durable savings capture. The selected distribution fraction credits initial margin retention, not unbounded mark-up economics.
research_memo.adoption_timing: The 68% agency-side display-programmatic figure exceeds the scorecard threshold, so t50 is zero under the frozen rule [S2]. It is a European, display-focused automation proxy rather than a US NAICS-541830 AI census, and programmatic buying predates generative AI. Still, it is strong evidence that automated execution is already operationally normal enough that a later 50% adoption date would be misleading.
research_memo.consolidation_economics: The target universe is thin in the supplied data, and large independents can dominate the statistics. Marketing Dive reports Empower and Ocean Media merged into a $1.5B-billings independent group, combining resources and buying power while maintaining operations [S5]. That demonstrates strategic scale benefits but is not a comp for a $1-10M EBITDA target. Commercial valuation guidance explicitly values media buyers on EBITDA on fees/net revenue rather than gross media spend [S6], making revenue-quality diligence non-negotiable.
research_memo.terminal_demand: Terminal demand is pressured rather than melting. Brands still need cross-platform allocation, attribution, data governance, negotiation and accountable advice, and US internet ad revenue reached $258.6B in 2024 [S3]. But the independent operator's historic execution layer is being automated by programmatic systems and platforms. The likely survivor is a smaller, data-capable advisory/buying specialist; a generalist whose offer is merely placing media is vulnerable to direct platform tools and fee compression.
research_memo.risks_and_uncertainty: The supplied 4-digit 5418 occupation mix contains obvious PR, design, promoter and display-role contamination, so the role estimate is explicitly adjusted and carries a large residual. The programmatic statistic is European and measures automation, not US firm-level AI adoption. The multiple source is commercial guidance rather than a disclosed transaction database, and reported receipts can include media pass-through. Diligence must reconcile gross billings to fee revenue, disclose principal/rebate economics, test client concentration and establish whether vendor relationships transfer.

evidence.reviewer_note: All six score-driving source URLs were reopened on 2026-07-21. Exact 541840 owner-age, succession, independent-target and transaction data were not found in the reopened public sources; those inputs are deliberately low-confidence estimates rather than presented as observed facts.

research_memo.executive_view: Media Representatives is a narrow, heterogeneous commission business: the Census definition is independent sale of media time or space for media owners, rather than an ad agency or a media-buying shop [S1]. The buy thesis is weak. AI can remove proposal, research, traffic and billing work, but its main economic consequence may be to make the owner’s represented inventory easier to sell through programmatic channels rather than to create a scarce new service. Katz, the large national incumbent, earns commissions on national spot and online advertising sold for station clients [S2]; that confirms both the commission model and the dependence on a media-owner client relationship. I classify terminal demand as PRESSURED rather than MELTING because the NAICS boundary includes online and specialty representation, but a buyer should underwrite a low multiple, contract-transfer risk, and a much smaller target universe than total establishments suggest.
research_memo.how_ai_changes_work: The supplied 5418 occupation mix is an imperfect proxy, so the scorecard does not treat its PR and creative shares as literal media-rep staffing. In an actual representative, AI is most useful in audience research, RFP responses, proposal assembly, avails packaging, order entry, trafficking, invoicing and reconciliation; relationship selling to stations, publishers and agency accounts remains the core human work. This is consistent with broader adoption being early rather than saturated: Census BTOS reported 17–20% of firms using AI in business functions from December 2025 through May 2026 and less than 20% for firms with four or fewer employees [S3]. The selected replaceable share is therefore a task estimate, not a claim that the code’s sales function disappears.
research_memo.value_capture: The operator charges through a commission structure, not a time-and-materials fee, so labor savings from proposal and traffic automation can initially remain with the representative [S2]. That favorable form is offset by weak bargaining power: the represented media owner can renegotiate commissions, sell more inventory directly, or expose inventory to programmatic channels. Comscore reported that CTV reached 28% of surveyed media budgets in 2025 and that nearly half of marketers increasing programmatic CTV budgets were reallocating from linear TV [S4]. This is a distinct capture channel from labor automation: it threatens the commission base and the owner’s relationship moat, not the task-share estimate.
research_memo.adoption_timing: I use 20% current effective adoption and four years to 50% effective adoption. BTOS is not a media-representative survey and measures any-business-function use, so it cannot prove workflow-level automation in this code [S3]. It does, however, establish that broad small-business adoption has not already crossed the 50% boundary. The four-year estimate assumes faster adoption in documents, research and order workflows than the all-business average, tempered by fragmented small firms, contract-specific traffic systems, and continuing human sales relationships.
research_memo.consolidation_economics: National representation is already concentrated: iHeart describes Katz as a media representation firm earning commissions from station advertising [S2]. That is an incumbent benchmark, not evidence that there is an active, acquirable roll-up lane among the 50 deterministic $1–10M EBITDA targets. The closest public lower-middle-market valuation proxy is media buying agencies, where the cited marketplace describes 4.5–6.0x EBITDA for well-run agencies and specifically warns that value is on net fee revenue rather than gross media spend [S5]. A small representative with terminable media-owner contracts and declining linear exposure deserves a discount to that proxy; the selected 3.5x buy and 3.75x exit are deliberately conservative estimates, not observed 541840 transaction multiples.
research_memo.terminal_demand: Paid demand survives in niches where a representative owns a useful local, political, specialty or digital selling relationship, but the code’s overall terminal is pressured. The Census scope expressly includes independent representatives selling time or space for media owners [S1], and iHeart’s filing shows the model still generates commission revenue [S2]. At the same time, digital advertising reached nearly $300 billion in 2025, up 13.9%, while programmatic CTV budgets were moving from linear TV [S4, S6]. AI makes the transaction more automatable; it does not create the represented media inventory or guarantee that a third-party rep retains the sale.
research_memo.risks_and_uncertainty: The central uncertainty is composition. NAICS 541840 includes radio, television, print, internet and other media representatives, while the public evidence largely describes a large broadcast incumbent and broad advertising markets [S1, S2]. The frozen role mix is at four-digit 5418 and materially contaminated by agency, PR and creative occupations, so actual role fractions and AI value could move sharply. There is no public, code-specific lower-middle-market transaction database or owner-age census in the sources reopened here. Multiples, ownership age, succession and active-consolidator inputs are accordingly low-confidence estimates with explicit proxy caveats; no investment decision should rely on them without a target-level contract, client-concentration and renewal review.

records.title: Indoor and Outdoor Display Advertising

publication_caveats: inputs.ai_replaceable_share: The supplied 4-digit 5418 workforce omits much installation, print, maintenance, permitting and lease work while overstating agency/PR roles.
inputs.ai_replaceable_share: Programmatic transaction growth is not counted as a V input; it is addressed in pi_dist and terminal discussion.
inputs.pi_dist: No reopened source reports the platform take rate or AI savings pass-through for independent 541850 operators.
inputs.pi_moat: Moat is asset-specific: short leases, non-transferable permits and poor locations can sharply reduce it.
inputs.t50_years: Neither source is a 541850 workflow-adoption survey.
inputs.current_adoption_pct: BTOS is all-business evidence and Place Exchange is a selected platform, not the full code.
inputs.historical_analogs: The source covers one platform’s patterns and must not be read as all-industry adoption.
inputs.owners_60plus_pct: S4 discusses plant valuation and owner-operated sales expense, not a representative owner-age survey.
inputs.owners_60plus_pct.succession_shortage_documented: Not documented is not evidence of no target-level succession need.
inputs.active_consolidators: Count is deliberately limited to an active acquirer documented in a reopened primary filing, not a census of all buyers.
inputs.buy_mult: S4 and S7 report billboard cash flow, not EBITDA; accounting definitions, lease treatment and sales-cost normalization can make the proxy materially wrong.
inputs.exit_mult: The source multiple is billboard cash flow, not EBITDA. Exit premium is not used in capture or terminal scoring.
inputs.pricing_structure: This describes billboard plants; 541850 also includes indoor, transit and display-design activities.

Census S1 opens as the NAICS search application without a rendered 541850 result, so the exact industry-scope text is not directly visible at the cited URL; S1 is not score-driving and the packet separately discloses the code's heterogeneous boundary.

The 24% of DOOH spend in 2024 figure in narrative.value_capture is not present on S5's exact page; S5 does support the cited 51% Place Exchange venue mix and platform-specific momentum. Because pi_dist is explicitly an estimate with a platform/pass-through caveat and does not depend on the 24% figure, this does not change C or the thesis.
review.summary: Mechanics, identity, digests, deterministic dataset injection, fresh finalization, memo rendering and arithmetic all pass. The physical-inventory moat, active-acquirer evidence and OOH-demand evidence support the useful durable-but-asset-specific thesis. The cash-flow-multiple and workforce proxies are prominently disclosed, and two non-material citation issues require publication caveats; neither demonstrates a likely factor, score or thesis reversal.

ai_replaceable_share.rationale: AI can automate sales-support, creative, scheduling, reporting and administration, but not the physical operating and site-control work that a true display operator carries. The supplied agency-weighted proxy requires a low-confidence reconstruction.
caveats: The supplied 4-digit 5418 workforce omits much installation, print, maintenance, permitting and lease work while overstating agency/PR roles.
Programmatic transaction growth is not counted as a V input; it is addressed in pi_dist and terminal discussion.

breakdown_by_role.rationale: Prospecting, proposals, quoting and availability lookups automate; local selling remains.
Communication and reporting work is AI-assistable, but this is agency-proxy contamination.

breakdown_by_role.role: Market Research Analysts and Marketing Specialists
breakdown_by_role.rationale: Audience packaging, reporting and research are highly assistable.

Promotional support is partly automatable but not core plant labour.

breakdown_by_role.role: Sales Representatives of Services, Except Advertising, Insurance, Financial Services, and Travel

Planning and analysis automate; pricing and commercial leadership persist.

pi_dist.rationale: Location rental economics and direct-cost cash-flow structure support savings retention, while programmatic platforms increase price transparency and transaction intermediation.
caveats: No reopened source reports the platform take rate or AI savings pass-through for independent 541850 operators.

pi_moat.rationale: Permitted locations, remaining lease terms, structures and advertiser relationships create a physical-asset moat; Lamar’s acquired site-location and structure assets corroborate their economic importance.
caveats: Moat is asset-specific: short leases, non-transferable permits and poor locations can sharply reduce it.

t50_years.rationale: Current broad AI use remains below 50%; office and creative workflows can move quickly, but field-heavy operations and fragmented operators slow effective adoption.
caveats: Neither source is a 541850 workflow-adoption survey.

current_adoption_pct.rationale: A 25% effective-adoption estimate modestly exceeds broad BTOS use because programmatic workflow penetration is visible, without treating programmatic buying as full firm AI adoption.
caveats: BTOS is all-business evidence and Place Exchange is a selected platform, not the full code.

historical_analogs.value: Programmatic OOH’s growing transaction infrastructure is a relevant analogue for automated selling and reporting, while the physical site asset remains outside the analogue.
historical_analogs.rationale: OAAA/Place Exchange documents platform transaction, inventory and deal-mix momentum rather than an AI-specific adoption curve.
historical_analogs.plausible_range: Automation may spread quickly in DOOH but remain limited in field operations and static plants.
caveats: The source covers one platform’s patterns and must not be read as all-industry adoption.

owners_60plus_pct.rationale: A 45% working estimate recognizes small operator ownership but is not reported by the reopened source.
caveats: S4 discusses plant valuation and owner-operated sales expense, not a representative owner-age survey.

succession_shortage_documented.rationale: No reopened source documents a code-level succession shortage, so this is conservatively false.
caveats: Not documented is not evidence of no target-level succession need.

active_consolidators.rationale: Lamar is one directly documented active consolidator: its 2025 10-K reports more than 50 outdoor-asset acquisitions plus Verde Outdoor.
active_consolidators.plausible_range: At least 1; complete private buyer count not established
caveats: Count is deliberately limited to an active acquirer documented in a reopened primary filing, not a census of all buyers.

buy_mult.rationale: Selected from reopened 10–12x and 12.5–14x standardized billboard-cash-flow discussions, using 12x as a conservative normalized-EBITDA proxy for a good lease-backed plant.
caveats: S4 and S7 report billboard cash flow, not EBITDA; accounting definitions, lease treatment and sales-cost normalization can make the proxy materially wrong.

exit_mult.rationale: A 13x EBITDA proxy assumes a buyer values a de-risked, lease-backed portfolio and realizes scale benefits; current Lamar acquisition activity supports an exit channel but not this exact multiple.
caveats: The source multiple is billboard cash flow, not EBITDA. Exit premium is not used in capture or terminal scoring.

pricing_structure.value: Advertising-rental revenue from display inventory, less direct plant costs including leases, permits, maintenance, utilities and sales cost
pricing_structure.rationale: The reopened industry source defines standardized billboard cash flow using advertising rental revenue and the listed direct costs.
pricing_structure.plausible_range: Pricing and lease burden vary materially by format, geography and asset quality
caveats: This describes billboard plants; 541850 also includes indoor, transit and display-design activities.

cross_checks.uplift_vs_A_consistent: A roughly 34% task-share estimate creates modest available uplift, while a four-year adoption estimate reflects that the most automatable work is not the whole physical operating model.

terminal_value.justification: Permitted, leased physical display inventory remains a paid asset while the full OOH market continued to grow; durability remains asset-specific.

cross_checks.no_proxy_confirmed: No. The 4-digit 5418 role mix, BTOS adoption data, full-OOH revenue statistics and billboard-cash-flow multiples are all disclosed proxies or broader-than-code evidence.
cross_checks.pricing_model_deflation: Location scarcity supports savings retention, but programmatic buying can increase rate transparency and platform intermediation; this pressure is represented in pi_dist rather than terminal classification.

evidence.reviewer_note: All seven score-driving source URLs were reopened on 2026-07-21. The multiple sources report standardized billboard cash flow rather than EBITDA; packet values therefore state an explicit proxy conversion caveat instead of asserting an observed EBITDA multiple.

research_memo.executive_view: Indoor and Outdoor Display Advertising is an asset-heavy advertising business, not an ad agency: Census defines it as creating display campaigns and/or placing displays on billboards, panels, transit, malls and other structures [S1]. Its durable feature is permitted, leased and built physical inventory. OOH revenue reached $9.46 billion in 2025, grew 3.6%, and digital OOH accounted for 36.3% of revenue [S2]. AI can materially lower sales-support, design and administrative cost but cannot replace structures, permits, landowner leases or field maintenance. I classify terminal demand as DURABLE, while retaining low overall confidence because NAICS 541850 spans billboard plants, transit, indoor, display-design and other formats with very different economics.
research_memo.how_ai_changes_work: The supplied 5418 role mix is agency-weighted, so it overstates PR, research and creative labour and omits much of the installation, printing, lease and field-operation tail. I use it only as the deterministic wage-share shell and judge actual tasks within it. AI can automate prospect lists, rate-card queries, proposal generation, proof-of-performance reports, billing, contract administration and creative iteration. Physical posting, structure maintenance, site permitting, lease negotiation and local selling remain partly or wholly human. BTOS’s 17–20% broad-business AI-use range shows adoption is early across firms, not already at a 50% effective-workflow threshold [S3].
research_memo.value_capture: A display operator sells scarce inventory rather than hours. Billboard cash-flow guidance defines the asset cash flow after direct costs such as leases, maintenance, utilities, permit fees and an imputed sales cost [S4]. This supports a high, though not perfect, share of labour savings remaining with the operator: site rents are set by location and demand, not directly by back-office labour. Programmatic buying adds price transparency and a platform relationship, but OAAA data still show a mixed market: programmatic was 24% of DOOH spend in 2024 and outdoor inventory was 51% of spend on the Place Exchange platform in H1 2025 [S5]. The platform channel is a capture risk, not an assumption that it destroys the physical asset.
research_memo.adoption_timing: I estimate current effective AI adoption at 25% and time to 50% at four years. BTOS provides a broad lower bound rather than a code-specific measurement [S3]. Adoption should be faster in creative, sales-support and billing teams, but the benefit is limited by smaller operators, disparate CMS and traffic systems, and the fact that most wage cost in a real plant includes field work that AI does not automate. Programmatic OOH is a related automation path, but it is a transaction technology, not proof that the whole operator has adopted AI [S5].
research_memo.consolidation_economics: There is real, current buyer activity. Lamar completed more than 50 outdoor-asset acquisitions for $191.1 million in 2025 and acquired Verde Outdoor for $147.6 million; the filing identifies site locations, structures, contracts and leases as acquired assets [S6]. That supports one documented active consolidator and a credible exit path for quality assets. The multiple benchmark must be handled carefully: Billboard Insider reports 10–12x standardized billboard cash flow for well-maintained plants with a reasonable remaining lease portfolio [S4], while its Clear Channel analysis discusses 12.5–14x billboard cash flow [S7]. Those are cash-flow, not EBITDA, measures. I use 12.0x buy and 13.0x exit as transparent EBITDA proxies with a wide range, not as claimed observed EBITDA comps.
research_memo.terminal_demand: The terminal is durable because AI cannot replicate a permitted display location, although media demand can still cycle and formats can lose share. OOH revenue grew for a nineteenth consecutive quarter through 2025, while traditional formats also posted gains and DOOH increased 10.5% [S2]. Lamar’s 2025 acquisitions and its allocation of consideration to site locations and advertising structures reinforce that scarcity has continuing economic value [S6]. A target with short leases, weak permits, poor locations or transit-management contracts is not protected by the industry result; terminal durability must be verified asset by asset.
research_memo.risks_and_uncertainty: The critical diligence risks are lease duration, permit status, structure condition, local demand, customer concentration, digital conversion capital and which portion of revenue is a physical plant rather than design or transit management. The deterministic target count uses a generic services margin and may undercount mature billboard plants; it is not changed here. The public multiple sources use standardized billboard cash flow rather than EBITDA, and OAAA statistics cover the full OOH medium including large public operators [S2, S4]. Owner age and succession evidence for independents is weak in reopened public sources, so those inputs remain low-confidence estimates.

current_adoption_pct.rationale: Effective adoption is set below 50% because reported usage is fragmented and organizational barriers remain despite widespread experimentation.

historical_analogs.value: Digital workflow adoption in printing is a relevant analogue: gains arrive through estimating, prepress and workflow integration, while physical production remains. Long-run electronic diversion is a separate demand headwind.

succession_shortage_documented.rationale: Practitioner commentary and the broad owner survey both support succession/exit planning as an issue, though not a quantified sector shortage.

active_consolidators.rationale: The direct-mail M&A source identifies active buyers and multiple direct-mail transaction/search examples; count only a small, observed strategic-buyer set rather than infer a broad sponsor universe.

caveats: This is a target-quality scenario, not a market-wide multiple; the cited transaction data are SDE-based and proxy-only.

pricing_structure.value: Competitive job-based print-and-mail pricing with postage/paper pass-through; differentiated data, compliance and fulfillment services can be stickier.

terminal_value.justification: Official Marketing Mail volumes have declined materially over the decade, but targeted, data-driven direct mail retains paid use cases. The terminal assessment is demand pressure, separate from whether AI savings are retained.

cross_checks.no_proxy_confirmed: No. The 5418 role mix, printing/SDE valuation cohort, broad owner survey and generic margin-based band count are disclosed proxies rather than exact 541860 evidence.
cross_checks.pricing_model_deflation: AI savings in creative, list/data and administration are rebiddable in standard jobs; the operator retains more only where data, compliance, fulfillment and client integration create switching friction.

evidence.reviewer_note: All six driving URLs were reopened on 2026-07-21. Multiples are intentionally low-confidence estimates anchored to real sold small-printing SDE data, with the metric and NAICS mismatch disclosed.

research_memo.executive_view: This is a selective, not broad, roll-up candidate. The exact NAICS scope covers campaign creation and preparation of materials for mailing or other direct distribution, and may include mailing-list work [S1]. Data-rich direct-mail operators can still be useful service businesses, but commodity print-and-mail production faces a shrinking physical-mail base and limited ability to retain AI savings. The appropriate posture is conditional diligence on data, regulated-customer and fulfillment capabilities rather than a thesis on generic mail production.
research_memo.how_ai_changes_work: AI is most useful in the white-collar layer: list hygiene, segmentation, copy and creative variants, estimating, prepress, job intake and customer communication. PRINTING United's 2025 study reports use in customer communications, estimating, prepress and workflow automation, while also describing fragmented adoption and organizational barriers [S3]. It does not replace presses, inserters, finishing equipment, postal induction or accountable plant operations. The supplied 5418 occupation mix is agency-heavy, so the residual is intentionally treated as production-heavy rather than as invisible office automation.
research_memo.value_capture: Savings are likely to be competed away in standard print-and-mail jobs: buyers can rebid data preparation, creative and administrative work while postage and paper remain outside AI's cost-cutting reach. A data-management, compliance or fulfillment workflow can retain more because it embeds client data, controls and operating integration; New Direction describes data-driven 1:1 mail houses as using targeted campaigns to offset postal-rate increases [S5]. That is a moat channel distinct from the distribution/pass-through channel, not a reason to inflate task automation.
research_memo.adoption_timing: The industry is past novelty but not at 50% effective operating adoption. The 2025 print-provider research says most participants had moved beyond experimentation, but only a small minority had a formal AI roadmap and the surveyed segments extend beyond direct mail [S3]. I use 30% current effective adoption and three years to 50% for viable operators; this is an estimate, not a sector census, and assumes adoption of estimating, prepress, data and service workflows rather than automation of physical production.
research_memo.consolidation_economics: There is real strategic activity, but it rewards differentiated operators. New Direction reports a direct-mail opportunity with about $6.0m sales and $0.9m EBITDA and identifies active buyers for direct-mail/mailing firms [S5]; its sector commentary says traditional commercial-print owners are often near retirement and that differentiated data-management and ancillary services improve attractiveness [S5]. BizBuySell's 2021-25 sold printing cohort traded at a 2.45x median owner-earnings multiple, not an EBITDA multiple [S4]. I therefore use conservative, explicitly proxy-based EBITDA inputs and do not model expansion as an AI benefit.
research_memo.terminal_demand: Terminal demand is pressured rather than assumed durable. USPS's official series shows Marketing Mail volume fell from 80.9bn pieces in FY2016 to 56.8bn in FY2025 [S2], and the USPS OIG attributes the long decline in Marketing Mail to diversion to lower-cost online alternatives [S2]. The decline is not a forecast that every specialized mailer disappears: NDP still identifies data-driven 1:1 direct mail as attractive [S5]. It does mean cost savings on a generic volume base should be valued as defense, not as a standalone growth engine.
research_memo.risks_and_uncertainty: The biggest uncertainty is classification and mix. Census distinguishes 541860 campaign/preparation work from 541870 physical delivery and 32311 printing [S1], while the deterministic role mix is 4-digit 5418 and omits many plant roles. The $1-10m target count uses a generic margin and should not be treated as a verified direct-mail target universe. The valuation evidence is sold small printing businesses measured on owner earnings/SDE, not exact-NAICS EBITDA; owner age is a broad small-business and sector-practitioner proxy. Customer concentration, data-security obligations, postage exposure, real-estate normalization and the actual share of data services require deal-level diligence.

evidence.reviewer_note: All seven driving URLs were reopened on 2026-07-21. The record deliberately does not manufacture a NAICS-specific consolidator count or EBITDA multiple from adjacent sources.

research_memo.executive_view: This is a pass for a broad roll-up thesis. Exact NAICS 541870 covers physical direct distribution or delivery of advertisements and samples, including door-to-door, windshield placement and retail handouts [S1]. AI can improve routing, reporting, quoting and administration, but it does not perform the core last-mile work. The remaining economic question is whether advertisers keep paying for a physical medium when digital alternatives and postal distribution options constrain price; that is a terminal-demand problem, not an AI-automation opportunity.
research_memo.how_ai_changes_work: AI assists the thin information layer: carrier-route targeting, route design, job quoting, proof-of-delivery exception handling, billing, payroll administration and client reporting. It cannot walk routes, hang pieces, deliver samples or staff an in-store demonstration, the activities Census uses to define this code [S1]. The supplied 5418 occupation proxy is severely agency-weighted, so its large residual is treated as field-distribution labor with a low automatable fraction. Print-industry AI evidence is used only as a broad workflow analogue, not as evidence of autonomous delivery [S6].
research_memo.value_capture: Distribution is likely to pass through a large portion of the limited office savings. USPS offers Every Door Direct Mail to target neighborhoods, cities and ZIP Codes [S3], creating a published alternative for a material portion of physical-advertising demand; a local distributor also competes against rebidding and against digital ad budgets. Reliable last-mile execution and evidence of delivery can preserve some service margin, but neither is an AI moat. Therefore low distribution retention and low moat inputs reflect price discipline, not a claim that AI removes the physical workforce.
research_memo.adoption_timing: Effective AI adoption is below 50% and likely slower than in direct-mail production because only a minority of the actual wage bill is addressable. The 2025 printing study documents AI moving into estimating, customer communication and workflow functions, but also fragmented implementation and significant organizational barriers [S6]. I use 20% current effective adoption and five years to 50% for the addressable operating layer. This measures adoption of tools in the operator, not automation of door-to-door or sampling labor.
research_memo.consolidation_economics: The deterministic band count is small and fragile: it rests on a generic 15.65% margin despite the code's labor-intensive physical-delivery definition. No verified, NAICS-specific consolidator count or completed-transaction multiple was located in the reopened public sources. The only available small-business valuation anchor is a sold printing/SDE cohort, which is a weak unit and sector proxy [S4]. Generic owner succession evidence is not enough to convert a small, highly local field-labor market into a roll-up thesis. Buy only where routes, client contracts, crew compliance and delivery verification are already proven.
research_memo.terminal_demand: Terminal demand is melting for a generalist advertising-material distributor. Marketing Mail volume fell from 80.9bn pieces in FY2016 to 56.8bn in FY2025 [S2], while the USPS OIG describes long-run electronic diversion toward lower-cost online alternatives [S2]. The physical channel is not zero: USPS reports nearly 3bn EDDM pieces in FY2025 [S5]. But that evidence supports persistence of a channel, not durable pricing or a growing pool of independent last-mile distributors; direct-distribution firms also depend on non-postal flyers and samples, so the exact melt rate is uncertain.
research_memo.risks_and_uncertainty: This code has unusually high boundary and data risk. Census separates 541870 physical delivery from 541860 campaign/preparation, 541890 specialties and 32311 printing [S1], but the deterministic role mix is the broad 5418 advertising group. The wage-share result could overstate automation if the unlisted residual is even more field-labor intensive than assumed. Multiples are not direct evidence: the source reports owner earnings/SDE for printing businesses, not EBITDA for advertising-material distribution. Contractor classification, route-density economics, client concentration, proof-of-delivery liability and the share of in-store sampling must be verified before any deal is underwritten.

review.summary: Mechanics, exact artifact identity, fresh finalization, memo rendering and arithmetic all pass. All nine registered sources were reopened; the score-driving sources support the limited claims made, and the packet openly treats promotional-products distribution, adoption, role mix and valuation as scoped proxies or estimates. The low-confidence kill thesis remains useful with all 13 authored caveats and no additional material or validator-authored defect.

cross_checks.uplift_vs_A_consistent: A roughly 2.5-year path to 50% effective adoption is consistent with meaningful but staged quote-to-cash automation rather than an immediate labor reset.

evidence.reviewer_note: Single-author initial fleet packet. All cited score-driving URLs were reopened on the stated access date; no code-specific owner-age survey or disclosed lower-middle-market transaction multiple was represented as observed.

research_memo.executive_view: This is a heterogeneous catch-all, but promotional-products distribution is the best-supported working dominant subsegment: Census product detail explicitly includes coordinating logoed advertising-specialty production and delivery [S2], while ASI reports a $26.6bn promo industry [S3]. A $1–10m EBITDA target can harvest meaningful quoting, artwork and order-administration savings, but it is not a clean software-like roll-up: supplier-side revenue is in the trade total, client books can be portable, and physical/event-service work is not replaced. I therefore view the code as a conditional relationship-protected reseller opportunity, not a broad AI windfall.
research_memo.how_ai_changes_work: AI primarily removes information and coordination work: product search, first-pass artwork, mockups, proof routing, quote preparation, store administration, supplier chasing and billing. ASI reports that 50% of extra-large ($5m+) distributors use AI and that creative copy is the most common use among AI users [S4], which supports rapid tool diffusion but not full task replacement at smaller firms. In-person demonstrations, sampling, installation and the physical production and delivery of merchandise remain human or supplier work; salespeople and account leaders still own the brief and relationship.
research_memo.value_capture: The economic model is markup resale and managed-program service, not a billable-hour consultancy. 4imprint's 2025 results show $1.347bn revenue, $436m gross profit and $145m operating profit [S5], demonstrating that scaled direct distribution can retain healthy economics. That is a benchmark, not a comp for a small independent: online self-service makes search, artwork and ordering more contestable and is likely to pass a material part of labor savings to buyers. I set capture below one-half on both distribution and moat, because company stores and supplier access help retention but portable sales books and transparent quotes limit it.
research_memo.adoption_timing: The reported 50% AI-use rate applies to extra-large distributors, not the full code or the lower-middle market [S4]. I therefore estimate current effective adoption at 35%, with roughly 2.5 years to 50% effective adoption as tools move from copy and mockups into quote-to-cash workflows. This is deliberately slower than casual AI use: integration with supplier data, approval workflows and client systems matters more than access to a general model.
research_memo.consolidation_economics: There is real, current consolidation: HALO acquired What's Happening Promotions in October 2025 [S6], BDA acquired Harper + Scott in September 2025 [S7], and Overture acquired Pride Products in January 2026 [S8]. These facts support active buyers, not a claim that every small distributor is buyable or that deal terms are known. The selected 4.0x buy and 5.0x exit multiples sit inside Windsor Drake's Q1 2026 marketing/digital-agency $3m–$10m EBITDA range of 4.0x–6.5x [S9], but that is an adjacent broad category; a target with concentrated or salesperson-owned accounts warrants the low end.
research_memo.terminal_demand: Demand for physical branded merchandise is more durable than the distributor's intermediation. Census describes a 541890 product as coordinating production of logoed specialties and delivering them to the client [S2], and AI cannot itself manufacture or distribute the object. The pressure is that AI-native search and self-service can reduce the value of the independent coordination layer. I classify terminal demand as PRESSURED rather than MELTING: enterprise program management, fulfillment and trusted account service can persist, but a bare-bones reseller has a credible bypass risk.
research_memo.risks_and_uncertainty: The code includes merchandise coordination, demonstrations and other advertising services, so no single trade statistic maps cleanly to it. ASI's $26.6bn figure covers a broader promotional-products ecosystem [S3], and its 50% AI statistic is for extra-large distributors [S4]. The deterministic OEWS proxy is 4-digit 5418 and imports agency roles, so the role estimate is sensitive. There is no verified, code-specific owner-age survey or disclosed small-target transaction multiple in the reopened sources; those inputs are deliberately low-confidence estimates rather than invented facts.

evidence.reviewer_note: Single-author initial fleet packet. Every cited score-driving URL was reopened on the stated access date. The packet does not represent code-specific ownership demographics or transaction terms as observed because the reopened sources did not provide them.

research_memo.executive_view: Marketing research and public-opinion polling systematically gathers, records, tabulates and presents marketing and opinion data under the official NAICS definition [S1]. AI can quickly reduce the cost of questionnaire drafting, coding, analysis, charting and report production, but this is a pressured rather than clean value-migration case: low-end studies face self-service and synthetic alternatives, while human samples, methodological accountability and trusted client counsel remain valuable. The deterministic occupation proxy is unusable because 4-digit 5419 is dominated by veterinary siblings, so this is a low-confidence investment screen rather than a staffing measurement.
research_memo.how_ai_changes_work: The immediate AI use cases are research design drafts, open-end coding, transcript summarization, tabulation assistance, analysis narratives and proposal writing. Greenbook's 2025 GRIT report describes AI adoption as a permanent market shift while emphasizing human-centered insights and a trust problem around 'good enough' work [S2]. The ICC/ESOMAR code separately defines a human individual in contrast to a synthetic person [S3], a useful reminder that AI can accelerate the workflow without necessarily substituting for the respondent or the accountable researcher. Senior methodology, recruitment for difficult samples and client decision support remain less replaceable.
research_memo.value_capture: Project work is often competitively bid, so savings from programming, coding and reporting are likely to be shared with clients rather than retained in full. Retainers, trackers, proprietary panels and regulated/high-consequence work can retain more value because the buyer is paying for continuity, quality control and defensible human data. I set distribution at 35% and moat at 40%: these are intentionally conservative judgments reflecting price competition and founder/key-researcher dependency, offset partly by trust, methods and client integration. They do not use the human-data floor to inflate capture; that floor is considered under terminal demand.
research_memo.adoption_timing: The reopened GRIT page establishes a strong qualitative industry shift, but it does not provide a public NAICS-wide effective-adoption percentage [S2]. I estimate current effective adoption at 45% and 2.5 years to 50%, distinguishing embedded workflow use from occasional generative-AI experimentation. That keeps t50 positive as required by the stated adoption estimate and allows for procurement, privacy, validation and client-trust constraints.
research_memo.consolidation_economics: Visible strategic consolidation exists but is tilted toward large data, panels and technology rather than the ordinary $1–10m EBITDA agency: Kantar completed its Numerator acquisition [S4], NIQ agreed to acquire Gastrograph AI [S5] and reports nine tuck-ins [S6], and WPP acquired InfoSum for AI-enabled marketing intelligence [S7]. These are evidence of strategic buyer interest, not comparable private-company terms. I use a 3.5x buy and 4.25x exit against Windsor Drake's adjacent 3.0x–4.5x $1m–$3m and 4.0x–6.5x $3m–$10m marketing/digital-agency ranges [S8]; key-person and substitution risks keep the estimate low-confidence.
research_memo.terminal_demand: Paid demand is not simply labor demand. The ESOMAR code defines primary data as information collected directly from or about an individual for research [S3], and its treatment of the human individual separately from a synthetic entity reflects a continuing quality and ethics distinction. Yet low-stakes exploratory work, survey programming and analysis can move to DIY tools or synthetic alternatives. I classify the code as PRESSURED: human panels, careful sampling and defensible interpretation can remain durable, but they are not guaranteed to protect a generic boutique from budget deflation or insourcing.
research_memo.risks_and_uncertainty: The code boundary excludes many data/analytics and DIY-platform businesses, while the large acquisitions cited here span technology, panels, consumer intelligence and marketing data rather than a clean US 541910 lower-middle-market sample. The supplied 4-digit 5419 role mix contains veterinary occupations and cannot identify research staffing; the residual role judgment is therefore a transparent modeling device. No reopened source supplied a code-specific owner-age distribution, succession shortage, or disclosed buy/exit multiple. Those values are estimates with explicit caveats, and polling headlines should not be generalized to the larger commercial-research segment.

publication_caveats: inputs.ai_replaceable_share: The source is a vendor-sponsored, AI-adopter survey rather than a representative US portrait-studio time-and-motion study.
inputs.ai_replaceable_share: Generative substitution of the purchased portrait is treated in terminal demand and capture, not as labor replacement.
inputs.pi_dist: Based on a vendor survey of AI adopters, not observed studio margin retention.
inputs.pi_moat: The record has no US revenue split between contracted volume work and retail/founder-led work.
inputs.t50_years: This is workflow adoption, not replacement of physical portrait capture.
inputs.current_adoption_pct: No representative US portrait-studio adoption census was found in the reopened sources.
inputs.owners_60plus_pct: Do not treat this estimate as evidence of a succession wave.
inputs.owners_60plus_pct.succession_shortage_documented: Absence of documentation is not proof that no owner will retire.
inputs.active_consolidators: The acquisition is historical and its target is not a typical $1-10M EBITDA portrait studio.
inputs.buy_mult: The score requests EBITDA multiples but the available photography evidence is SDE-based marketing guidance, not closed $1-10M EBITDA transactions; this is an imperfect proxy.
inputs.exit_mult: The same SDE-versus-EBITDA mismatch applies; the Lifetouch transaction is a non-comparable national-volume benchmark.
inputs.pricing_structure: No code-wide revenue split is available in the reopened sources.
The registered Census 541921 URL currently resolves to the official NAICS search interface without exposing the cited definition in its static page text.
review.summary: Mechanics and exact-artifact binding pass. Reopened evidence supports workflow automation, weak retention/transferability, thin consolidation economics, and a pressured but not wholly melting demand profile. The kill thesis remains useful, with all authored limitations preserved and one additional non-fatal caveat about the exact Census URL's current static rendering.

pi_dist.rationale: Some studios can sell speed or experience, but price sensitivity and fragmented competition pass much of the saving through.
caveats: Based on a vendor survey of AI adopters, not observed studio margin retention.

pi_moat.rationale: School contracts are a defensible niche, but most local portrait client books and owner-led style are weakly transferable.
caveats: The record has no US revenue split between contracted volume work and retail/founder-led work.

t50_years.rationale: AI post-production tools are mature and visible to adopters, but representative penetration is not observed.
caveats: This is workflow adoption, not replacement of physical portrait capture.

current_adoption_pct.rationale: A substantial minority estimate reflects visible AI-adopter workflow use without mistaking an adopter survey for market penetration.
caveats: No representative US portrait-studio adoption census was found in the reopened sources.

historical_analogs.value: Digital galleries and online ordering shifted delivery and package sales before generative AI; AI is a faster post-production layer with a separate product-substitution risk.
historical_analogs.rationale: The analogy is used to distinguish operational digitization from the more disruptive generated-image channel.
historical_analogs.plausible_range: Operational digitization is a partial, not complete, analogue

owners_60plus_pct.rationale: No reopened source provided a code-specific owner-age distribution; the value is a deliberately weak placeholder rather than a claimed fact.
caveats: Do not treat this estimate as evidence of a succession wave.

succession_shortage_documented.rationale: No code-specific documentation of a succession shortage was found in reopened sources.
caveats: Absence of documentation is not proof that no owner will retire.

active_consolidators.rationale: Lifetouch/Shutterfly evidences one national volume-photography consolidator; it does not establish a broad small-studio buyer set.
caveats: The acquisition is historical and its target is not a typical $1-10M EBITDA portrait studio.

buy_mult.rationale: A 2.2x entry is placed within the reopened 1.5-3.0x photography SDE range, discounted for thin targets and owner dependence.
caveats: The score requests EBITDA multiples but the available photography evidence is SDE-based marketing guidance, not closed $1-10M EBITDA transactions; this is an imperfect proxy.

exit_mult.rationale: Exit is set slightly below entry because AI and founder-transfer risk do not create a credible small-studio rerating thesis.
caveats: The same SDE-versus-EBITDA mismatch applies; the Lifetouch transaction is a non-comparable national-volume benchmark.

pricing_structure.value: Session/event fee plus print or package upsells; school photography adds contracted package sales.
pricing_structure.rationale: Census scope and the Lifetouch transaction support a mix of portrait/event and school-volume channels.
pricing_structure.plausible_range: Mix varies materially by operator
caveats: No code-wide revenue split is available in the reopened sources.

cross_checks.uplift_vs_A_consistent: Moderate workflow adoption can arrive quickly, but the low capture assumptions reflect competition and separate product substitution rather than a claim that task automation is slow.

terminal_value.justification: Physical occasion-led portrait work retains paid demand, while output-only headshots and simple portraits have a direct generated-image substitute.

cross_checks.no_proxy_confirmed: No: owner age, SDE-based valuation evidence, vendor survey selection and segment mix are disclosed proxies or limitations.
cross_checks.pricing_model_deflation: AI savings are likely divided among faster delivery, premium service and customer price pressure; the 70% reported price-sensitivity result is why pi_dist is modest. [S2]

top_uncertain_inputs.score_impact: A faster path raises A but does not cure low capture or terminal-demand risk.
Whether a target owns renewable school contracts versus a founder's local book materially changes C.
The lack of EBITDA-specific small-studio transactions makes M directionally but not precisely supported.

evidence.reviewer_note: All five score-driving URLs were reopened on 2026-07-21. No claim of representative AI adoption, owner age, or closed EBITDA multiples is made where the reopened sources do not support it.

research_memo.executive_view: Portrait photography is a poor lower-middle-market roll-up candidate despite useful workflow automation. The Census scope includes school, passport, home, wedding and special-event portrait work [S1], but the supplied target count leaves only 21 firms in the inferred $1-10M EBITDA band. AI can reduce the editing and administration burden; it does not make a founder-dependent local client book, or the physical capture at a school or event, transferable. [S2][S4]
research_memo.how_ai_changes_work: The near-term change is concentrated after the shoot: AI culls, applies a first edit, retouches, drafts client messages and speeds delivery. Aftershoot's survey of more than 1,000 AI-adopting photographers reports that its users use faster delivery tiers and that 60% are emphasizing premium experiences, although that vendor-sponsored sample is not a penetration survey. [S2] The photographer still has to secure the booking, direct people in person, operate the shoot and approve the final aesthetic result.
research_memo.value_capture: A small studio can retain some saved post-production time through faster turnaround or a higher-touch experience, but low barriers and client price sensitivity limit retention. The same survey says 70% of respondents saw increased price sensitivity and 60% leaned into premium experiences rather than price cuts [S2]; that supports modest, not zero, capture. The cost-side score deliberately excludes the larger threat that a consumer can substitute a generated headshot or image for a conventional output.
research_memo.adoption_timing: Effective use of AI editing is already established among active adopters, but a majority-of-industry estimate remains uncertain because the available survey is self-selected and covers photographers globally rather than US portrait studios. I estimate 35% current effective workflow adoption and about 1.5 years to 50%, primarily for culling, editing and communication, rather than for on-site image capture. [S2]
research_memo.consolidation_economics: This code is structurally bifurcated. Shutterfly acquired Lifetouch, described as the national leader in school photography, for $825m in 2018 [S5], while a typical small photography business is marketed at 1.5-3.0x seller's discretionary earnings, with owner dependence and transferable systems central to value [S4]. Those are not directly comparable valuation bases. The thin supplied target band, owner labor embedded in SDE, and founder-owned client relationship make the buyability case weak.
research_memo.terminal_demand: Paid demand is PRESSURED rather than melting outright. School, sports, newborn, wedding and other occasion-led shoots require physical presence and coordination, and PPA's 2025 consumer study still finds affluent recent headshot buyers willing to pay for a professional experience [S3]. Output-only headshots and simple portraits face a more direct generative substitute. The record should therefore not extrapolate resilient physical occasions to the whole NAICS scope.
research_memo.risks_and_uncertainty: The largest uncertainty is mix: 541921 spans school, passport, home, wedding and special-event portrait work [S1], each with a different relationship owner and substitution risk. Employer data omit much of the independent/founder fringe, while the supplied labor share may understate owner labor. The multiple evidence is SDE-oriented marketing guidance rather than closed, $1-10M-EBITDA portrait-studio transactions, so M is deliberately conservative and low confidence. [S4]

review.summary: All deterministic mechanics, identity, route, dataset, fresh-finalization, memo, digest, and arithmetic checks pass. All seven registered score-driving sources were reopened and provide useful directional support, but the evidence is mostly broader than NAICS 541922 and does not directly quantify several selected estimates. No material defect was found; the kill thesis remains publishable with the authored caveats and the added non-fatal terminal-demand evidence caveat.

labor_share.method: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 541922 ($721,209,516) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 541922 ($2,481,739,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

ai_replaceable_share.rationale: Assistive AI materially automates editing, culling, file preparation and administration, but physical capture, creative direction and client-facing production remain partly human. Fractions measure technically automatable task share, not layoffs or demand substitution.
caveats: Supplied role mix is 5-digit 54192 and is shared with portrait studios; retail and process shares need not represent commercial studios.
Fractions separate task automation from terminal demand; generative substitution is assessed in terminal value rather than used to inflate V.
The residual wage bucket likely contains production assistants and digital technicians whose mix differs by commercial subsegment.

breakdown_by_role.rationale: Editing, retouching, compositing and delivery automate; directing and capturing a real subject remain physical and creative work.
Scheduling, estimates, metadata and routine client administration are workflow-automation candidates.
Bid preparation, scheduling and studio operations automate partly; account judgment and production leadership remain.

breakdown_by_role.role: Photographic Process Workers and Processing Machine Operators
breakdown_by_role.rationale: File preparation, processing and repeatable delivery work are highly automatable in a digital workflow.
